<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136715794 space=MD2024x version=2 -->
## PAGE 00626: Guidelines for Working with Large Models

- page_id: `136715794`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715794/Guidelines+for+Working+with+Large+Models
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-07-01T10:58:21.967+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: UsageInDiagrams is Deprecated

2033054156

INLINE

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: UsageInDiagrams is Deprecated

2033054168

INLINE

2033054154

INLINE

**On this page**

**42**

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: UsageInDiagrams is Deprecated

2033054158

INLINE

Product performance can be highly dependent on the model organization strategy. From long-term work experience with various clients, we have selected the biggest challenges and gathered their possible solutions in one place.

##### Teamwork Cloud Operations SpecificsTeamwork Cloud Operations Specifics

###### **Project Opening**

Opening Teamwork Cloud project for the first time may take a bit longer, as all project-related data has to be downloaded from the server and then processed in the modeling client. The download time depends on the project size and network speed, e.g., for large projects (containing 5M/3.5M model elements in "19.0 and before/2021x and later" version), it may require downloading 1GB or more of data. Once the project is opened, we store your project data locally. The processing time depends on the downloaded project size. Opening the project for the second time is much faster as there is no need to request all data from the server again. Instead, the previously downloaded and locally stored data is reused. Only new information has to be downloaded from the server. The maximum processed data count is specified by the **Recent Files List Size** environment option. The processed project data is always preserved when the project is saved offline. 
More information can be found [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Opening projects from Teamwork Cloud' space='']. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Open Server Project dialog.

###### Committing

Frequent commits make it easier for other team members to track project changes; however, this should be avoided if many users are working on a particular project. Frequent commits create a lot of project versions and increase the commit time due to the need to update the project every time. You need to decide upon the best-committing strategy within your company. A widely used practice is to commit the changes at the end of the work day; however, this should be avoided because, in some cases, the project commit may take longer than expected. 
More information can be found [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Committing changes to Teamwork Cloud' space=''].

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Switching Used Project versions

Teamwork Cloud projects are usually built from smaller parts. Different teams simultaneously develop different parts of the project, so there is a need to keep the model up-to-date. A user is notified when a new version of the used project arrives, and it is up to the user whether to apply the update or not. As not all used project version updates are important, there is no need to update the version each time a new version arrives. Therefore, it is best to set up a version update strategy within the teams. You can decide whether or not to switch to a new used project version by considering the severity of that version – the commit tags can be used to mark that the particular version contains the major changes and should be used instead. 
More information can be found [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Changing the used project version' space=''].

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Notification about version changes.

In some cases, the project version update may take longer than expected due to a local cache update.

**Used Projects Auto Update Plugin**. This plugin allows users to set up specific used projects to update. We recommend that you schedule it to launch at night so that the latest versions will be ready to use the next work day. More information can be found [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Used Projects Auto Update Plugin' space=''].

###### Merge

To perform a merge operation, the ancestor and contributor (source and target) projects have to be loaded, meaning that the memory consumption increases at least two times compared to the memory required to perform daily tasks in the project. After testing the memory consumption with different-sized projects, the following minimum memory requirement for the merge operation was identified: *2 x (memory required to load project) + 1 GB** .*These memory requirements are indicative and may vary by project. It strongly depends on the number of detected changes between merged versions (in this case, the project merge was done with 1.5k changes in total).

Since the merge operation uses a high amount of memory, please make sure that your machine is capable of performing this task. If the required memory exceeds the maximum amount of memory that can be allocated to the tool, the dedicated hardware should be used instead. In case of excessive memory usage, the merge operation can be optimized for lower memory consumption by excluding differing diagrams/elements count (**Optimize for** > **Memory** in the **Merge Projects** dialog); however, this optimization slows down the merge operation. 
More information can be found [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Model merge in Teamwork Cloud' space=''].

###### Project Decomposition

Model partitioning enables a more granular model management. Despite the benefits of granularity, project decomposition should be done carefully since managing used projects can become a bottleneck from a performance perspective. Please follow the criteria below to decompose a project:

- 20+ active users work on the same project simultaneously. As a result, the project contains many project versions, which slow down project history-related operations. Also, a higher number of users leads to a higher number of commits, resulting in longer update times and slowing down the commit operation.
- A large number of models owned by the same person indicates that the model granularity is too small or there is a need to distribute responsibilities.
- The part of the project (such as type libraries) should be used in other projects.
- Different teams work on a certain aspect of the system separately. All these separate parts are then connected in the integration project.

###### Publishing projects to Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud

As publishing greatly depends on the element count and diagram complexity (e.g., diagram type, the number of elements displayed, dynamic or static content, etc.), please consider the publishing scope before publishing. Since publishing uses a high amount of memory, please make sure that your machine is capable of performing this task. Remember that if the required memory exceeds the maximum amount of memory that can be allocated to the tool, the dedicated hardware should be used instead. 
More information can be found [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Publishing Cameo Collaborator documents' space='CC4TWC2024x'].

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Publishing a model is the first step in the workflow for reviewing models and providing feedback.

###### Content History

If retrieving a particular element history takes a lot of time, it implies that the Content History action should be performed carefully as it retrieves the history of all contained elements at once. Before invoking this action, you should carefully consider the scope of the elements contained by the element for which the action is invoked. 
More information can be found [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Reviewing content history' space=''].[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### History of element changes in the scope of a *Book*package.

##### Teamwork Cloud Operations Troubleshooting

**Waiting for Response**

If the operation that requires a remote call to Teamwork Cloud is taking too long, you can analyze a *.log* file to learn how much time was spent processing the operation on the client and server-side.

Where do I find a .*log* file?

1. Open your modeling tool.
2. In the main menu, go to Help > About <modeling tool name> .
3. In the Environment tab, click the file path next to Log File to open a *.log* file.

**Measuring Network Latency**

*INFO NetworkPerformance - <operation name> latency <latency time>*

**Description**

The latency (i.e., the time it takes for the ping signal sent from the tool to the server to return) is measured for the following operations:

- Login
- Committing project to server
- Updating project
- Opening project

Example: INFO NetworkPerformance - Login latency 1 ms

**Remote Operations Duration Statistics**

*INFO RemoteCallsAnalyzer - <operation name> all operations time <total operation time>*

*INFO RemoteCallsAnalyzer - <operation name> calls total time: <total time, spent in remote calls>, start count: <remote calls count>, failed count: <failed remote calls count>*

**Description**

The remote and local operations duration statistics are collected for the following operations:

- Updating from local project
- Opening Manage Projects dialog
- Opening Open Server Project dialog
- Updating project
- Committing changes to a server project
- Setting selected version as latest
- Cloning project
- Managing version properties
- Opening Teamwork Cloud project
- Renaming project
- Removing project
- Adding project to server
- Viewing element history
- Comparing projects
- Using server project
- Discarding changes
- Locking and unlocking elements/diagrams
- Changing used project version

Example:

- INFO RemoteCallsAnalyzer - Manage projects all operations time 9797 ms
- INFO RemoteCallsAnalyzer - Manage projects calls total time: 2293 ms, start count: 47, failed count: 0

##### Common Features

###### Validation

The validation engine helps to keep the model accurate, complete, and correct. Since model checking is a complicated task that greatly depends on the project size, please consider the following recommendations to keep the modeling tool running smoothly:

- Set the Validation Scope to narrow down the list of validated elements.
- Set the Validate Only Visible Diagrams project option to true to limit a validation scope to visible diagrams only. Since 2021x version, all projects are migrated to validate only visible diagrams. This is a key option to keep the modeling tool running smoothly while working on large models.
- Set the Exclude Elements from Used Read-Only Project project option to true to skip the validation of elements used projects contain. [ATTACHMENT filename='validation_project_options.png']

###### Querying the Model

Ineffectively written queries can cause showstopper problems, especially when a project becomes quite large. The most attention should be paid to Script, Find, and Find Usage in Diagrams operations. Be aware that the slow execution of queries can noticeably impact the modeling experience - some operations trigger an expression (e.g., smart packages and derived properties) recalculation.

- The Find operation should not be performed for the whole project scope when specifying expressions for smart packages, derived properties, validation rules, etc. Since the operation is highly dependent on the search scope - the wider it is, the longer it takes - it can noticeably impact the modeling experience by slowing down the common modeling tool commands that, as a result, cause the expression recalculation.
- It is not a good practice to use Find for searching common elements (e.g., Class, Package, Block, etc.).
- Searching element usages in diagrams should be used carefully since the operation searches for element usages in dynamic content. UsageInDiagrams is DeprecatedThis operation is very expensive. It not only checks symbolic diagrams for elements but also loads and builds Generic Tables, Dependency Matrices, Relationship Maps, etc. It should be best avoided and used only if you understand the possible consequence of building all diagrams in the project.Instead of it, please use the **UsageInDiagramsWithSymbols**operation****that searches for usages only in symbolic diagrams. Other diagrams, such as Generic Tables, Dependency Matrices, Relationship Maps, etc., are not searched. This operation performs fast and does not load any diagrams to get the information.
- Smart Package specifics:
  - How does this affect performance? The Smart Package content is calculated on demand and cached. Next time on content request, the cached value is used. The cache is cleared on any model change. So the impact on performance depends on the performance of the content calculation, content request frequency, and model change frequency. Examples :
    - Model Browser: if a Smart Package is visible in the Model Browser, it will be refreshed each time after the cache is invalidated (model change occurs, e.g., a new element is created or an existing element is removed). So if a Smart Package is added directly under the root, it will always be visible in the Containment Tree (after opening a project, the root is expanded by default).
    - Validation: if the Smart Package is within the validation scope and the "Exclude elements from additional content" option is disabled, running validation will recalculate Smart Packages if the cache is invalidated.
    - Suspect links: Show Suspicions action triggers the calculation of Smart Packages that fall within the Tracking Scope. Starting with 2021x, the calculation of Smart Packages is no longer triggered.
    - Tables/matrices: if the Smart Package is within the table/matrix scope, it will be calculated by default. Starting with 2021x Refresh1, the content of Smart Packages is not included by default - the Include Additional Content table/matrix option was introduced for that.
  - The Use In Selection Dialogs option - set to false if a slow-running query is defined. [ATTACHMENT filename='Use_In_Selection_Dialogs_option.png']
  - Avoid nesting Smart Packages, i.e., using one Smart Package as part of another Smart Package's content.
  - Freezing the contents of a Smart Package . If you do not want the contents of the Smart Package to be updated automatically, you can suspend the calculation of the Smart Package contents. As a result, elements gathered according to a set of criteria specified in the Query dialog are no longer automatically added to the Smart Package contents. You can undo this at any time by changing the Frozen state.

The chart below shows how the Legend item condition execution time differs by performing the same expression in different languages. We advise choosing the most efficiently performing languages which are indicated as recommended (StructuredExpression, Groovy).

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A chart comparing the Legend item condition execution time by language.

###### **Troubleshooting**

You can use the Evaluate Operation Performance capability to see the performance diagnostics that help determine the reasons behind slow tool operations if they are caused by the execution of expressions (e.g., derived properties, smart packages, legends, validation rules) defined in the model. Matrix and table building can also be analyzed.

For example, you noticed that opening a particular diagram took longer than expected. A progress bar titled "Adorning" popped up for a few seconds before the diagram opened. To know what caused this performance issue, run the Evaluate Operation Performance tool. In its dialog, click the 'Start' button, thenopen the diagram again reproducing the slow operation, and onceit is done, click the 'Stop' button to see the results. You can use this information to investigate the particular expression and improve its efficiency.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

More information can be found [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Evaluating operation performance' space=''].

###### **Diagrams**

The consumed memory increases while working with the project for some time. One of the reasons is the number of open diagrams. As high memory usage becomes a problem, we recommend that you do not keep unused diagrams open. To prevent keeping a large number of open diagrams, beginning with version 2021x, the open diagrams limit was introduced with 20 diagrams set as the default value.

Please find below how memory consumption increases depending on the size of a diagram/table:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Chart depicting memory consumption by diagram size (symbols/rows displayed in a diagram/table).

- The matrix/table building time greatly depends on the specified scope - the wider it is, the more time it takes to build the matrix/table.
- Please avoid setting the entire model as the scope even if the matrix/table building performs well, as it can cause unacceptable performance in the following circumstances:
  - a project containing such a table/matrix is used in another project;
  - the model evolves over time, so the table will eventually reach unscalable sizes.
- Also, it is not advisable to choose more than 10 element types to be displayed because this may lead to a decrease in the matrix/table building time. Since matrices/tables have an option to define a scope query, the Smart Packages should not be set as scopes as well.

###### Working with Large Tables

The process of calculating all data in large tables (containing thousands of rows) can be slow and inefficient. For the most efficient performance, we suggest the following:

- Avoid creating tables that exceed ±50 000 cells as that can affect the performance of actions performed on the table data; in such a case, it is recommended to create multiple tables instead.
- Switching to partial loading. Data is loaded only in the visible area, so the most common tasks are performed quickly. More information can be found [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Partial loading in tables' space=''] . [ATTACHMENT filename='partial_loading_options.png']

###### Working with Model Browser

The organization of the model is determined by the package hierarchy you create to partition the elements in the model into logically cohesive groups. This affects the model browser performance as well - to avoid sluggish browsing in Containment Tree, you need to organize your model to avoid 10k and more directly owned elements by the particular element. Since the containment model browser is a lazy tree, it calculates and represents the nodes while expanding the owner. A large number of owned elements can cause unexpected waiting time to complete the task. So, we recommend splitting elements by creating different packages.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A model structured into packages.

##### Virtual Environments

- Two issues have been identified in VMWare ESX environments. To help prevent problems on the Teamwork Cloud side, we have come up with recommended hardware configurations.

1. 
  1. Overcommitting of resources . The same host has an over-allocation of CPU/RAM. The bigger problem is the over-allocation of RAM. For example, let's say a machine has 1TB of physical RAM. You allocate more than 1TB across various VM's in the host. What happens is that the RAM is no longer physical RAM, but rather mapped to disk, so performance decreases. Xen (Citrix) and KVM do not allow you to over-commit. In these, you can allocate min/max but never exceed the physical RAM, and as more instances start using up the RAM, the system reduces the allocated memory.
  2. Thin disk provisioning . This arises from an over-allocation of disk resources. Let's say you have a given amount of disk space to be used among VM's. In many cases, the admin does not know how much the actual disk usage is required per machine. Some VM's may use more, some less. Therefore, you over-allocate the storage using thin provisioning. Under thin provisioning, only the amount of storage being used is allocated to the virtual disk. As utilization increases, the virtual disk (which is a file) grows. This, however, has a negative impact on the performance of input/output-intensive applications like databases. For Cassandra, it is a killer in performance because compaction can potentially temporarily double the on-disk size. All disks must be thick provisioned.

- The other item is that, at the very least, you need to be using SAS-SSD. If using SAS-SSD, the host needs to have high-end caching controllers. On the Teamwork Cloud side, the core count is important - the more cores, the better.
- SAN's are not recommended. Only NVMe (all flash storage) will provide the necessary performance for large installations, provided that they are properly configured and that enough bandwidth is allocated to the instances.
- It is recommended that you stay away from Windows deployments. There is no freely available Cassandra Windows installer.
- If we look at cloud hosting, the older instances in Amazon use the Xen hypervisor (xcp), whereas the new ones use the kvm hypervisor. These are the instances you should use: preferably, the NVMe-based i3 instances, next would be r5, and finally m5. What is critical is to provision the filesystem properly.
- On the client side, the same issues of resources over-allocation can be a performance killer. Thin provisioning affects performance because disk caches are used extensively. Therefore, what is more, important when using VDI's is working on machines that have faster processors. When provisioning the VDI's, we do not recommend that you allocate a lot of CPU's because they will not be used. It is important to have the fastest processors.

##### Antivirus

Since some antivirus software can cause a significant performance decrease upon opening a project and performing other actions, we recommend that you exclude the MagicDraw home directory from the antivirus scanning scope.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="81e95404-cab0-41c7-b545-2047469b80a6"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">2033054156</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="47231aad-b8da-4fe1-90e5-190a99247b87"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">2033054168</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f8a2bb67-6f28-4baf-86e0-c8ef7704238a"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">2033054154</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="89bd7402-235e-4b97-9cb0-5a6430439222"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">4</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="minLevel">2</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></strong></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d6d2c4d4-d6dc-453b-9b02-0bf099bf87a4"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">2033054158</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Product performance can be highly dependent on the model organization strategy. From long-term work experience with various clients, we have selected the biggest challenges and gathered their possible solutions in one place.</p><h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5e2b8224-4af5-41e7-bad9-16cf4775b4fa"><ac:parameter ac:name="">Teamwork Cloud Operations Specifics</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>Teamwork Cloud Operations Specifics</h3><h4><strong>Project Opening</strong></h4><p>Opening Teamwork Cloud project for the first time may take a bit longer, as all project-related data has to be downloaded from the server and then processed in the modeling client. The download time depends on the project size and network speed, e.g., for large projects (containing 5M/3.5M model elements in &quot;19.0 and before/2021x and later&quot; version), it may require downloading 1GB or more of data. Once the project is opened, we store your project data locally. The processing time depends on the downloaded project size. Opening the project for the second time is much faster as there is no need to request all data from the server again. Instead, the previously downloaded and locally stored data is reused. Only new information has to be downloaded from the server. The maximum processed data count is specified by the <strong>Recent Files List Size</strong> environment option. The processed project data is always preserved when the project is saved offline. <br />More information can be found <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Opening projects from Teamwork Cloud" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[here]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="open_server_project.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The Open Server Project dialog.</h6><h4>Committing</h4><p>Frequent commits make it easier for other team members to track project changes; however, this should be avoided if many users are working on a particular project. Frequent commits create a lot of project versions and increase the commit time due to the need to update the project every time. You need to decide upon the best-committing strategy within your company. A widely used practice is to commit the changes at the end of the work day; however, this should be avoided because, in some cases, the project commit may take longer than expected. <br />More information can be found <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Committing changes to Teamwork Cloud" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[here]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Committing is necessary for other team members to see the changes you've made " ac:alt="Committing is necessary for other team members to see the changes you've made "><ri:attachment ri:filename="working_scheme.png" /></ac:image></p><h4>Switching Used Project versions</h4><p>Teamwork Cloud projects are usually built from smaller parts. Different teams simultaneously develop different parts of the project, so there is a need to keep the model up-to-date. A user is notified when a new version of the used project arrives, and it is up to the user whether to apply the update or not. As not all used project version updates are important, there is no need to update the version each time a new version arrives. Therefore, it is best to set up a version update strategy within the teams. You can decide whether or not to switch to a new used project version by considering the severity of that version – the commit tags can be used to mark that the particular version contains the major changes and should be used instead. <br />More information can be found <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Changing the used project version" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[here]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Update_used_projects.PNG" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Notification about version changes.</h6><p>In some cases, the project version update may take longer than expected due to a local cache update.</p><p><strong>Used Projects Auto Update Plugin</strong>. This plugin allows users to set up specific used projects to update. We recommend that you schedule it to launch at night so that the latest versions will be ready to use the next work day. More information can be found <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Used Projects Auto Update Plugin" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[here]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><h4>Merge</h4><p>To perform a merge operation, the ancestor and contributor (source and target) projects have to be loaded, meaning that the memory consumption increases at least two times compared to the memory required to perform daily tasks in the project. After testing the memory consumption with different-sized projects, the following minimum memory requirement for the merge operation was identified: <em> <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">2 x (memory required to load project) + 1 GB*</span> </em> <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">.<br /></span>*These memory requirements are indicative and may vary by project. It strongly depends on the number of detected changes between merged versions (in this case, the project merge was done with 1.5k changes in total).</p><p>Since the merge operation uses a high amount of memory, please make sure that your machine is capable of performing this task. If the required memory exceeds the maximum amount of memory that can be allocated to the tool, the dedicated hardware should be used instead. In case of excessive memory usage, the merge operation can be optimized for lower memory consumption by excluding differing diagrams/elements count (<strong>Optimize for</strong> &gt; <strong>Memory</strong> in the <strong>Merge Projects</strong> dialog); however, this optimization slows down the merge operation.<br />More information can be found <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Model merge in Teamwork Cloud" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[here]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><h4><span style="font-weight: 600;letter-spacing: -0.003em;">Project Decomposition</span></h4><p>Model partitioning enables a more granular model management. Despite the benefits of granularity, project decomposition should be done carefully since managing used projects can become a bottleneck from a performance perspective. Please follow the criteria below to decompose a project:</p><ul><li><p>20+ active users work on the same project simultaneously. As a result, the project contains many project versions, which slow down project history-related operations. Also, a higher number of users leads to a higher number of commits, resulting in longer update times and slowing down the commit operation.</p></li><li><p>A large number of models owned by the same person indicates that the model granularity is too small or there is a need to distribute responsibilities.</p></li><li>The part of the project (such as type libraries) should be used in other projects.</li><li>Different teams work on a certain aspect of the system separately. All these separate parts are then connected in the integration project.</li></ul><h4>Publishing projects to Cameo Collaborator for Teamwork Cloud</h4><p>As publishing greatly depends on the element count and diagram complexity (e.g., diagram type, the number of elements displayed, dynamic or static content, etc.), please consider the publishing scope before publishing. Since publishing uses a high amount of memory, please make sure that your machine is capable of performing this task. Remember that if the required memory exceeds the maximum amount of memory that can be allocated to the tool, the dedicated hardware should be used instead. <br />More information can be found <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CC4TWC2024x" ri:content-title="Publishing Cameo Collaborator documents" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[here]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="main_workflow_publishing.png" /></ac:image></span></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Publishing a model is the first step in the workflow for reviewing models and providing feedback.</h6><h4>Content History</h4><p style="margin-left: 0.0in;text-align: justify;">If retrieving a particular element history takes a lot of time, it implies that the Content History action should be performed carefully as it retrieves the history of all contained elements at once. Before invoking this action, you should carefully consider the scope of the elements contained by the element for which the action is invoked. <br />More information can be found <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Reviewing content history" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[here]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.<ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Content_history.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">History of element changes in the scope of a <em>Book </em>package.</h6><h3>Teamwork Cloud Operations Troubleshooting</h3><p><strong>Waiting for Response</strong></p><p>If the operation that requires a remote call to Teamwork Cloud is taking too long, you can analyze a <em>.log</em> file to learn how much time was spent processing the operation on the client and server-side.</p><p class="title">Where do I find a .<em>log</em> file?</p><ol><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Open your modeling tool.</span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">In the main menu, go to </span><strong>Help</strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> &gt;</span> <strong>About &lt;modeling tool name&gt;</strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">.</span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">In the </span><strong>Environment </strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">tab, click the file path next to</span> <strong>Log File </strong> <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">to open a <em>.log</em> file.</span></li></ol><p><strong>Measuring Network Latency</strong></p><p><em>INFO  NetworkPerformance - &lt;operation name&gt; latency &lt;latency time&gt; </em></p><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>The latency (i.e., the time it takes for the ping signal sent from the tool to the server to return) is measured for the following operations:</p><ul><li>Login</li><li>Committing project to server</li><li>Updating project</li><li>Opening project<br /><br /></li></ul><p class="title">Example: INFO  NetworkPerformance - Login latency 1 ms</p><p><strong>Remote Operations Duration Statistics</strong></p><p><em>INFO  RemoteCallsAnalyzer - &lt;operation name&gt; all operations time &lt;total operation time&gt;</em></p><p><em>INFO  RemoteCallsAnalyzer - &lt;operation name&gt; calls total time: &lt;total time, spent in remote calls&gt;, start count: &lt;remote calls count&gt;, failed count: &lt;failed remote calls count&gt;</em></p><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>The remote and local operations duration statistics are collected for the following operations:</p><ul><li>Updating from local project</li><li>Opening<strong> Manage Projects</strong> dialog</li><li>Opening <strong>Open Server Project</strong> dialog</li><li>Updating project</li><li>Committing changes to a server project</li><li>Setting selected version as latest</li><li>Cloning project</li><li>Managing version properties</li><li>Opening Teamwork Cloud project</li><li>Renaming project</li><li>Removing project</li><li>Adding project to server</li><li>Viewing element history</li><li>Comparing projects</li><li>Using server project</li><li>Discarding changes</li><li>Locking and unlocking elements/diagrams</li><li>Changing used project version</li></ul><p class="title">Example: </p><ul><li>INFO  RemoteCallsAnalyzer - Manage projects all operations time 9797 ms</li><li>INFO  RemoteCallsAnalyzer - Manage projects calls total time: 2293 ms, start count: 47, failed count: 0</li></ul><h3>Common Features</h3><h4>Validation</h4><p>The validation engine helps to keep the model accurate, complete, and correct. Since model checking is a complicated task that greatly depends on the project size, please consider the following recommendations to keep the modeling tool running smoothly:</p><ul><li>Set the <strong>Validation Scope</strong><em> </em>to narrow down the list of validated elements.</li><li>Set the <strong>Validate Only Visible Diagrams</strong><em> </em>project option to <em>true </em>to limit a validation scope to visible diagrams only. Since 2021x version, all projects are migrated to validate only visible diagrams. This is a key option to keep the modeling tool running smoothly while working on large models.</li><li>Set the <strong>Exclude Elements from Used Read-Only Project </strong>project option to <em>true </em>to skip the validation of elements used projects contain.<br /><br /><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="validation_project_options.png" /></ac:image></li></ul><h4>Querying the Model</h4><p style="margin-left: 0.0cm;text-align: justify;">Ineffectively written queries can cause showstopper problems, especially when a project becomes quite large. The most attention should be paid to Script, Find, and Find Usage in Diagrams operations. Be aware that the slow execution of queries can noticeably impact the modeling experience - some operations trigger an expression (e.g., smart packages and derived properties) recalculation. </p><ul><li>The Find operation should not be performed for the whole project scope when specifying expressions for smart packages, derived properties, validation rules, etc. Since the operation is highly dependent on the search scope - the wider it is, the longer it takes - it can noticeably impact the modeling experience by slowing down the common modeling tool commands that, as a result, cause the expression recalculation.</li><li><p>It is not a good practice to use Find for searching common elements (e.g., Class, Package, Block, etc.).</p></li><li><p>Searching element usages in diagrams should be used carefully since the operation searches for element usages in dynamic content.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3d7dd41f-de74-4add-be1c-69c023c0427f"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">UsageInDiagrams is Deprecated</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p style="text-align: left;">This operation is very expensive. It not only checks symbolic diagrams for elements but also loads and builds Generic Tables, Dependency Matrices, Relationship Maps, etc. It should be best avoided and used only if you understand the possible consequence of building all diagrams in the project.</p><p style="text-align: left;">Instead of it, please use the <strong style="text-align: left;">UsageInDiagramsWithSymbols </strong>operation<strong style="text-align: left;"> </strong>that s<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">earches for usages only in symbolic diagrams. Other diagrams, such as Generic Tables, Dependency Matrices, Relationship Maps, etc., are not searched. This operation performs fast and does not load any diagrams to get the information.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li>Smart Package specifics:<br /><ul><li><strong>How does this affect performance? </strong>The Smart Package content is calculated on demand and cached. Next time on content request, the cached value is used. The cache is cleared on any model change. So the impact on performance depends on the performance of the content calculation, content request frequency, and model change frequency.<br /><strong>Examples</strong>:<ul><li>Model Browser: if a Smart Package is visible in the Model Browser, it will be refreshed each time after the cache is invalidated (model change occurs, e.g., a new element is created or an existing element is removed). So if a Smart Package is added directly under the root, it will always be visible in the Containment Tree (after opening a project, the root is expanded by default).</li><li>Validation: if the Smart Package is within the validation scope and the &quot;Exclude elements from additional content&quot; option is disabled, running validation will recalculate Smart Packages if the cache is invalidated.</li><li>Suspect links: <strong>Show Suspicions</strong> action triggers the calculation of Smart Packages that fall within the Tracking Scope. Starting with 2021x, the calculation of Smart Packages is no longer triggered.</li><li>Tables/matrices: if the Smart Package is within the table/matrix scope, it will be calculated by default. Starting with 2021x Refresh1, the content of Smart Packages is not included by default - the <strong>Include Additional Content</strong> table/matrix option was introduced for that.</li></ul></li><li>The <strong>Use In Selection Dialogs</strong> option - set to <em>false</em> if a slow-running query is defined.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Use_In_Selection_Dialogs_option.png" /></ac:image></li><li>Avoid nesting Smart Packages, i.e., using one Smart Package as part of another Smart Package's content.</li><li><strong>Freezing the contents of a Smart Package</strong>. If you do not want the contents of the Smart Package to be updated automatically, you can suspend the calculation of the Smart Package contents. As a result, elements gathered according to a set of criteria specified in the <strong>Query</strong> dialog are no longer automatically added to the Smart Package contents. You can undo this at any time by changing the <strong>Frozen</strong> state. <br /><ac:image ac:alt="frozen_enabled.png"><ri:url ri:value="https://docs.nomagic.com/download/attachments/70410160/frozen_enabled.png?version=1&amp;modificationDate=1616502291005&amp;api=v2" /></ac:image></li></ul></li></ul><p>The chart below shows how the Legend item condition execution time differs by performing the same expression in different languages. We advise choosing the most efficiently performing languages which are indicated as recommended (StructuredExpression, Groovy).</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Scripting_languages_performance.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><span style="color:var(--ds-background-accent-gray-bolder,#626f86);">A chart comparing the Legend item condition execution time by language.</span></h6><h4><strong>Troubleshooting</strong></h4><p>You can use the Evaluate Operation Performance capability to see the performance diagnostics that help determine the reasons behind slow tool operations if they are caused by the execution of expressions (e.g., derived properties, smart packages, legends, validation rules) defined in the model. Matrix and table building can also be analyzed.</p><p><span>For example, you noticed that opening a particular diagram took longer than expected. A progress bar titled &quot;Adorning&quot; popped up for a few seconds before the diagram opened. To know what caused this performance issue, run the Evaluate Operation Performance tool. In its dialog, click </span> <span>the 'Start' button, then </span><span>open the diagram again reproducing the slow operation, and once </span><span>it is done, click the 'Stop' button to see the results. </span> <span>You can use this information to investigate the particular expression and improve its efficiency.</span></p><p class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Evaluating_operation_performance.png" /></ac:image></p><p class="auto-cursor-target">More information can be found <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Evaluating operation performance" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[here]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><h4><strong>Diagrams</strong></h4><p style="margin-left: 0.0cm;">The consumed memory increases while working with the project for some time. One of the reasons is the number of open diagrams. As high memory usage becomes a problem, we recommend that you do not keep unused diagrams open. To prevent keeping a large number of open diagrams, beginning with version 2021x, the open diagrams limit was introduced with 20 diagrams set as the default value.</p><p style="margin-left: 0.0cm;">Please find below how memory consumption increases depending on the size of a diagram/table:</p><p style="margin-left: 0.0cm;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="memory_consumption_of_tables_and_diagrams_with_symbols.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="margin-left: 0.0cm;text-align: center;">Chart depicting memory consumption by diagram size (symbols/rows displayed in a diagram/table).</h6><ul><li>The matrix/table building time greatly depends on the specified scope - the wider it is, the more time it takes to build the matrix/table. </li><li style="list-style-type: none;"><p>Please avoid setting the entire model as the scope even if the matrix/table building performs well, as it can cause unacceptable performance in the following circumstances:</p><ul><li>a project containing such a table/matrix is used in another project;</li><li>the model evolves over time, so the table will eventually reach unscalable sizes.</li></ul></li><li>Also, it is not advisable to choose more than 10 element types to be displayed because this may lead to a decrease in the matrix/table building time. Since matrices/tables have an option to define a scope query, the Smart Packages should not be set as scopes as well.</li></ul><h4>Working with Large Tables</h4><p>The process of calculating all data in large tables (containing thousands of rows) can be slow and inefficient. For the most efficient performance, we suggest the following:</p><ul><li>Avoid creating tables that exceed ±50 000 cells as that can affect the performance of actions performed on the table data; in such a case, it is recommended to create multiple tables instead.</li><li>Switching to partial loading. Data is loaded only in the visible area, so the most common tasks are performed quickly. More information can be found <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Partial loading in tables" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[here]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="partial_loading_options.png" /></ac:image></li></ul><h4>Working with Model Browser</h4><p class="p1">The organization of the model is determined by the package hierarchy you create to partition the elements in the model into logically cohesive groups. This affects the model browser performance as well - to avoid sluggish browsing in Containment Tree, you need to organize your model to avoid 10k and more directly owned elements by the particular element. Since the containment model browser is a lazy tree, it calculates and represents the nodes while expanding the owner. A large number of owned elements can cause unexpected waiting time to complete the task. So, we recommend splitting elements by creating different packages.</p><p class="p1"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="model_browser_structure.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;margin-left: 0.0cm;">A model structured into packages.</h6><h3>Virtual Environments</h3><ul><li>Two issues have been identified in VMWare ESX environments. To help prevent problems on the Teamwork Cloud side, we have come up with recommended hardware configurations.</li></ul><ol><li style="list-style-type: none;background-image: none;"><ol><li><strong>Overcommitting of resources</strong>. The same host has an over-allocation of CPU/RAM. The bigger problem is the over-allocation of RAM. For example, let's say a machine has 1TB of physical RAM. You allocate more than 1TB across various VM's in the host. What happens is that the RAM is no longer physical RAM, but rather mapped to disk, so performance decreases. Xen (Citrix) and KVM do not allow you to over-commit. In these, you can allocate min/max but never exceed the physical RAM, and as more instances start using up the RAM, the system reduces the allocated memory.</li><li><strong>Thin disk provisioning</strong>. This arises from an over-allocation of disk resources. Let's say you have a given amount of disk space to be used among VM's. In many cases, the admin does not know how much the actual disk usage is required per machine. Some VM's may use more, some less. Therefore, you over-allocate the storage using thin provisioning. Under thin provisioning, only the amount of storage being used is allocated to the virtual disk. As utilization increases, the virtual disk (which is a file) grows. This, however, has a negative impact on the performance of input/output-intensive applications like databases. For Cassandra, it is a killer in performance because compaction can potentially temporarily double the on-disk size. All disks must be thick provisioned.</li></ol></li></ol><ul><li>The other item is that, at the very least, you need to be using SAS-SSD. If using SAS-SSD, the host needs to have high-end caching controllers. On the Teamwork Cloud side, the core count is important - the more cores, the better.</li><li>SAN's are not recommended. Only NVMe (all flash storage) will provide the necessary performance for large installations, provided that they are properly configured and that enough bandwidth is allocated to the instances.</li><li>It is recommended that you stay away from Windows deployments. There is no freely available Cassandra Windows installer.</li><li>If we look at cloud hosting, the older instances in Amazon use the Xen hypervisor (xcp), whereas the new ones use the kvm hypervisor. These are the instances you should use: preferably, the NVMe-based i3 instances, next would be r5, and finally m5. What is critical is to provision the filesystem properly.</li><li>On the client side, the same issues of resources over-allocation can be a performance killer. Thin provisioning affects performance because disk caches are used extensively. Therefore, what is more, important when using VDI's is working on machines that have faster processors. When provisioning the VDI's, we do not recommend that you allocate a lot of CPU's because they will not be used. It is important to have the fastest processors.</li></ul><h3>Antivirus</h3><p>Since some antivirus software can cause a significant performance decrease upon opening a project and performing other actions, we recommend that you exclude the MagicDraw home directory from the antivirus scanning scope.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136714913 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00628: Hide Metatype

- page_id: `136714913`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136714913/Hide+Metatype
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:16:57.153+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > UML Profiling and DSL Guide > Domain Specific Language customization > Creating Customization Data > Customization Elements
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Indicates whether the customized stereotype is recognized as a new type. If the **Hide Metatype** property value is *false*, the DSL element is *not* considered as a new type element and will not be displayed in the shortcut menus (see the **Possible Owners** property).

To define the hide metatype property

1. In the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Customizing Specification Window' space='MD2024x']**Customization** Specification window, click the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Customization Target' space='MD2024x']**Customization Target** property and then select a customization target.
2. Set the **Hide Metatype** property to *true*.
3. Reopen the project.

#### PANEL: Related Pages

Related Pages

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating Customization Data' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Using Customization Data' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Customization Elements' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Customization Element Properties Description' space='MD2024x']

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating your First Customization' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating and specifying derived properties' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Defining expressions' space='MD2024x']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136711763 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00629: Hide Uncommon Elements filter

- page_id: `136711763`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136711763/Hide+Uncommon+Elements+filter
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:13:54.144+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Working with model elements > Selecting elements
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1827084503

1827084505

1827084504

You can enable the *Hide Uncommon Elements* filter in the *Select Type* dialog to leave out uncommon element types and thus find the ones you need easier.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Hide Uncommon Elements filter in the Select Type dialog

Once *Hide Uncommon Elements*is enabled, the**filter returns only those elements whose types are based on:

- Class (except Metaclass and Term)
- Component
- Signal
- Interface
- DataType
- Enumeration
- PrimitiveType
- AssociationClass

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Specified property type

If the SysML profile is loaded, only elements of*Block*and*ValueType*types are listed in the *Select Type* dialog.

If the Stereotype tag types are being specified, metaclasses are not filtered out.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Metaclasses in the *Select Type* dialog

1827084502

**Related Pages:**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Selecting port and part types' space='MD2024x']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136713326 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00630: Hiding packages

- page_id: `136713326`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136713326/Hiding+packages
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:15:27.327+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Collaborative modeling > Using Teamwork Cloud
- labels: ['hidden-packages', 'non-hidden-part', 'non-hidden-model', 'non-hidden-project', 'synchronization', 'manual-synchronization', 'automatic-synchronization', 'stripping-project', 'reusing-model', 'reusing-project']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Example

1191527483

INLINE

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Example

1191527485

INLINE

1333798886

INLINE

**On this page**

33

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Example

1191527484

INLINE

The Hiding packages utility lets you specify which model[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Package' space='MD2024x']elements within the model packages, and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Managing project usages' space='MD2024x'] should be hidden. Once specified, the public version of a model will be created by cutting/deleting the hidden parts. You can strip your project to reuse only a particular part of the project in another project. To strip your project, you must hide packages you do not want to share.

700500

##### Hiding packages

To hide packages

1. Open a server project.
2. Do one of the following:
  - Right-click the package you want to hide. On the shortcut menu, click Project Usages > Hide Packages .
  - On the main menu, click Collaborate > Hidden packages .
3. The **Hidden Packages** dialog opens. In the **Tree/List**select the packages you want to hide and****add them to the **Hidden Packages**side. Move the selected package back to the **Tree/List**side to unhide it. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
4. Click **OK** when you are done.
5. The [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Analyzing usages and dependencies' space='MD2024x'] is suggested. Usage checking may be time-consuming; however, it helps to identify elements that are related to the soon-to-be hidden parts . If any issues are found, they are displayed and described in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Usages and dependencies search results window' space='MD2024x']**Package Usage window** .
6. After you complete or skip Usage checking, you will see a notification that hiding the selected elements will be completed only after committing your project.
7. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Committing changes to Teamwork Cloud' space='MD2024x'] the project.
8. The stripped model version is created to be available to use in other projects. Hidden packages are marked with a lock sign [ATTACHMENT filename='hidden_package_notation.png'] in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Model Browser' space='MD2024x'] .

##### Hiding and unhiding used projects

You can hide/unhide a used project the same way as other packages. When hiding a used project, you cannot hide separate used project parts: you need to hide the whole used project.After hiding is done, a dedicated branch is created with the stripped model version, whereall the elements of the used project are hidden from the Containment tree due to being unused, and all references to these elements are cut from other model places.

To hide an indirectly used project, you need to hide it in the directly used project first.

#### TIP: Example

Example

Let's say you have a project A that uses a project B, and this project uses a project C. You want to hide the project C inside the project A. In this case, you need to hide it in the project B directly first and then use the public branch of the project B.

Indirectly used projects can also be hidden/unhidden automatically.Typically, this happens when the status of the used project changes:

- when the used project is hidden/unhidden
- when the used project is updated
- when the used project is removed
- when the new used project is used
- when used or using projects are merged
- when the used project is exported
- when the used project is imported

To hide an indirectly used project automatically, you need to hide all directly used projects that use the specific indirectly used project.

#### TIP: Example

Example

Let's say you have a project A that uses a project B, and this project uses a project C. You hide the project B and then the project C is hidden as well; however, if you add a new usage D that also uses C, the hidden usage of the project C is automatically unhidden because not all directly used projects using the project C are hidden in this case.

##### Dedicated branch for stripped model

A stripped model is stored in a dedicated branch created when element hiding is performed for the first time. By default, this branch is named *Public Project*. You can [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Branching projects' space='MD2024x'] if you need to.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Project with it`s Public Project branch. The public project now can be reused in another project.

If all packages from the model are unhidden, the dedicated branch will be deactivated.

If the main project is using a public branch of another project and that branch gets deactivated, you will be informed about this change with a warning icon in the main project with a warning icon [IMAGE alt='' src=''] in:

- Containment tree (under Project Usages root),
- Project Usages dialog,
- Advanced Project Usages Configuration dialog.

The state of the used project is checked at the same time interval as checking for a new project version in the background, or by clicking the Project Usages Update button.

##### Hidden package changes

After a package or a used project is successfully hidden or unhidden, an icon with a lock on a package (or used project) is shown in a Difference viewer.

The icon with a lock on a package (or on a used project package) is also shown in:

- Upcoming Changes browser panel,
- Local changes browser panel,
- Commit dialog,
- Content History panel,
- Element History.

##### Model synchronization

You must have the read-write permissionfor the stripped model branchto synchronize it.

A stripped model is read-only. It can only be used for reuse in other projects, not for editing (it can only be modified by synchronizing it – read-write permission is required for the stripped model branch). It can only be used for reuse in other projects, not for editing. However, you can edit the main project. On committing, checking is performed to see if the changes affect hidden and public parts of the model, and model synchronization is initialized. In the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Setting project options' space='MD2024x']**Project Options**dialog, you can define the model synchronization mode. The default mode is **Manual**; that is, after committing project changes, you will be notified about local changes affecting a public model stored in a dedicated branch. You can then decide either to synchronize changes or not.

The server project with hidden packages (and public branches) might get out of sync with the current version of the model in the public branch after set as the latest functionality in the **History** dialog is used. Therefore, you will be asked to perform hidden package synchronization.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

To select a synchronization mode

1. On the main menu, click Options > Projects . The Project Options dialog opens.
2. In the list of option groups, select General and then select Collaboration in the list of options.
3. For the option Synchronization Mode , specify one of the following values:

- 
  - Automatic - to synchronize changes automatically on committing the project changes.
  - Manual (default) - to synchronize changes upon your decision.
  - None - do not perform any synchronization.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Selecting a synchronization mode to specify how changes in the main project are synchronized to a branch where a stripped model version is stored

1191527482

INLINE

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Branching projects' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Identifying Package Dependencies' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Committing changes to Teamwork Cloud' space='MD2024x']

browser panel,</li><li>Local changes browser panel,</li><li>Commit dialog,</li><li>Content History panel,</li><li>Element History.</li></ul><h3>Model synchronization</h3><ac:structured-macro ac:ref="ee458cf5-7fd9-42d7-ba66-5bccef748f14">You</ac:inline-comment-marker> must have the read-write permission</span><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> for the stripped model branch </span><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">to synchronize it. </span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>A stripped model is read-only. It can only be used for reuse in other projects, not for editing (it can only be modified by synchronizing it – read-write permission is required for the stripped model branch). It can only be used for reuse in other projects, not for editing. However, you can edit the main project. On committing, checking is performed to see if the changes affect <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="bff5d5da-d58a-46fc-a1c0-bfe139277cd6">hidden and public</ac:inline-comment-marker> parts of the model, and <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="6d73fb86-dba4-4bc2-b359-51b0b40eda60">model</ac:inline-comment-marker> synchronization is initialized. In the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Setting project options" /><ac:link-body><strong>Project Options </strong>dialog</ac:link-body></ac:link>, you can define the <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="5cdbe521-8cae-47be-a959-afdbfa78efcd">model</ac:inline-comment-marker> synchronization mode. The default mode is <strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Manual</strong>; that is, <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="eeb6f786-be7e-472d-a58a-1bf81c497ccb">after</ac:inline-comment-marker> committing project changes, <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="ec088f7a-de5d-4145-b477-9562b3af9a0f">you will be notified</ac:inline-comment-marker> about local changes affecting a <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="4e001a51-fa61-4e5a-a17a-b1caa8b3ef17">public model</ac:inline-comment-marker> stored in a dedicated branch. You can then decide either to synchronize changes or not.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0f1f7b51-8f8d-4527-b1e7-67b4c991d290"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The server project with hidden packages (and public branches) might get out of sync with the current version of the model in the public branch after set as the latest functionality in the <strong>History</strong> dialog is used. Therefore, you will be asked to perform hidden package synchronization.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Hidden_packages_out_of_sync.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Hiding packages" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>To select a synchronization mode</p><hr /><ol><li>On the main menu, click <strong>Options</strong> &gt; <strong>Projects</strong>. The <strong>Project Options </strong>dialog opens.</li><li>In the list of option groups, select <strong> General</strong> and then select <strong>Collaboration </strong>in the list of options.</li><li>For the option <strong>Synchronization Mode</strong>, specify one of the following values:</li></ol><ul><li style="list-style-type: none;background-image: none;"><ul><li><strong>Automatic</strong> - to synchronize changes automatically on committing the project changes.</li><li><strong>Manual </strong>(default) - to synchronize changes upon your decision.</li><li><strong>None</strong> - do not perform any synchronization.</li></ul></li></ul><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="synchronization_mode.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Hiding packages" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><span style="color: rgb(112,112,112);">Selecting a synchronization mode to specify how changes in the main project are synchronized to a branch where a stripped model version is stored</span></h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="53f7957a-7599-4786-9733-1999a9bbb8d0"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1191527482</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Branching projects" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Identifying Package Dependencies" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Committing changes to Teamwork Cloud" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136712957 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00631: Hierarchy Diagram Wizard

- page_id: `136712957`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136712957/Hierarchy+Diagram+Wizard
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:15:10.642+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Model analysis > Model Visualizer
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This functionality is available in Standard, Professional, Architect, and Enterprise editions only.

The **Hierarchy Diagram Wizard** collects the largest hierarchies and allows each of them to be displayed either as separate diagrams or all in one diagram.

To create a diagram using the **Hierarchy Diagram Wizard**

1. Open the Hierarchy Diagram Wizard by doing one of the following:
  - On the Diagrams menu, select Diagram Wizards > Hierarchy Diagram Wizard .
  - From the Analyze menu, select Model Visualizer . In the open Model Visualizer dialog, select to open the Hierarchy Diagram Wizard and click **Start** .
2. Follow the steps as directed by the wizard.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="33df9e1d-7457-4043-ac81-6afd9c3c69da"><ac:rich-text-body><p>This functionality is available in Standard, Professional, Architect, and Enterprise editions only.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The <strong>Hierarchy Diagram Wizard</strong> collects the largest hierarchies and allows each of them to be displayed either as separate diagrams or all in one diagram.</p><p> </p><p>To create a diagram using the <strong>Hierarchy Diagram Wizard</strong></p><hr /><ol><li>Open the <strong>Hierarchy Diagram Wizard</strong><strong> </strong>by doing one of the following:<br /><ul><li>On the <strong>Diagrams</strong> menu, select <strong>Diagram Wizards</strong> &gt; <strong>Hierarchy Diagram Wizard</strong>.</li><li>From the <strong>Analyze</strong> menu, select <strong>Model Visualizer</strong>. In the open Model Visualizer dialog, select to open the <strong>Hierarchy Diagram Wizard</strong> and click <strong><strong>Start</strong></strong>.</li></ul></li><li>Follow the steps as directed by the wizard.</li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136710785 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00633: How aspects match with parts and ports

- page_id: `136710785`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136710785/How+aspects+match+with+parts+and+ports
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:13:00.980+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagramming > Diagram aspects
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The diagram aspects mechanism identifies whether or not the aspect name and name of the stereotypes applied for the parts and ports (or their types) match. The stereotype name must contain a matching aspect name. For example, a diagram aspect name is *Electrical,*which matches a port stereotype name W*Electrical.* When these conditions are met, you can display only the *Electrical* part of your model in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Composite Structure diagram' space='MD2024x']. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying parts and ports according to aspects' space='MD2024x']>]]>

If the diagram aspect name doesn't match the name of the part/port stereotype or its type, you can [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Stereotype' space='MD2024x'] with the same name as the aspect's and [apply it for parts and ports](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Stereotype#Stereotype-Applyingastereotype). Or you can [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating custom aspects' space='MD2024x'].

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Matching the diagram aspect *Electrical* with the port stereotype name *Electrical*.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>The diagram aspects mechanism identifies whether or not the aspect name and name of the stereotypes applied for the parts and ports (or their types) match. The stereotype name must contain a matching aspect name. For example, a diagram aspect name is <em>Electrical, </em>which matches a port stereotype name W<em>Electrical.</em> When these conditions are met, you can display only the <em>Electrical</em> part of your model in the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Composite Structure diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Composite Structure Diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Displaying parts and ports according to aspects" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[How to display parts and port according to aspect >>]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4704f5b7-46ac-40ca-a191-e433fd556328"><ac:rich-text-body><p>If the diagram aspect name doesn't match the name of the part/port stereotype or its type, you can <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Stereotype" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[create a custom stereotype]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> with the same name as the aspect's and <a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Stereotype#Stereotype-Applyingastereotype">apply it for parts and ports</a>. Or you can <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Creating custom aspects" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[create a custom diagram aspect]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="aspects_and_stereotype_match.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="How aspects match with parts and ports" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Matching the diagram aspect <em>Electrical</em> with the port stereotype name <em>Electrical</em>.</h6>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136714554 space=MD2024x version=3 -->
## PAGE 00634: How modeling tool adds its elements to velocity context

- page_id: `136714554`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136714554/How+modeling+tool+adds+its+elements+to+velocity+context
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2024-02-01T14:29:25.755+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard > Template Creation Tutorial > Linking report templates to the modeling tool
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1358598954

1358598955

1358598955

In Velocity, whenever you want to reference to a Java object you need to manually add the object to the Velocity template and name it. In Report Wizard, however, this process is done automatically. What you need to understand is how it is done as it will help you to call the modeling tool elements.

All elements in the modeling tool such as Class, Use Case, and Activity are the sub-classes of elements. During report generation, the report engine will get all the elements from a project within a selected scope that you have defined in Report Wizard, and then classify and add them to a list of the same type. The list will then be added to the Velocity Context with its type as its name. For instance, in a project where there are two types of Element, Class, and Use Case, all the elements that are identified as Classes will be kept on the same list and the list will be added to the Velocity Context and labeled as "Class" during report generation. This process also applies to the Use Case elements. To reference to a specific Class element, first you will have to make a reference to **$Class** (list of Class elements), and then iterate through the list to find that particular Class element by using the **#foreach**, **#forrow**, **#forcol**, or **#forpage** directive, followed by the **#if** directive to search for the name of the class.

This section will use part of the "*Inventory Control System.mdzip*" file in *<install.root>/samples/casestudies* as an example to show you how to reference elements.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Use Case View.

You need to know the elements inside the "Use Case View" package before using it, see figure above. To do this, follow the instructions in the previous section, *[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating Your First Template' space='']*.

The "Use Case View" package contains three element types: (i) Actor, (ii) UseCase, and (iii) Diagram that you can call in a template by typing **"$Actor**," **"$UseCase**," and **"$Diagram**" respectively.

In this example you will create a report that will print the names of the Actors, UseCases, and diagram image.

1358598953

**Related pages**

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

In Report Wizard, however, this process is done automatically. What you need to understand is how it is done as it will help you to call the modeling tool elements.</p><p>All elements in the modeling tool such as Class, Use Case, and Activity are the sub-classes of elements. During report generation, the report engine will get all the elements from a project within a selected scope that you have defined in Report Wizard, and then classify and add them to a list of the same type. The list will then be added to the Velocity Context with its type as its name. For instance, in a project where there are two types of Element, Class, and Use Case, all the elements that are identified as Classes will be kept on the same list and the list will be added to the Velocity Context and labeled as &quot;Class&quot; during report generation. This process also applies to the Use Case elements. To reference to a specific Class element, first you will have to make a reference to <strong>$Class</strong> (list of Class elements), and then iterate through the list to find that particular Class element by using the <strong>#foreach</strong>, <strong>#forrow</strong>, <strong>#forcol</strong>, or <strong>#forpage</strong> directive, followed by the <strong>#if</strong> directive to search for the name of the class.</p><p>This section will use part of the &quot;<em>Inventory Control System.mdzip</em>&quot; file in <em>&lt;install.root&gt;/samples/casestudies</em> as an example to show you how to reference elements.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="Use Case and Actor" ac:alt="Use Case and Actor"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddavb053991ffb02adecec8c94bef76403bd.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="margin-left: 30.0px;text-align: center;">Use Case View.</h6><p>You need to know the elements inside the &quot;Use Case View&quot; package before using it, see figure above. To do this, follow the instructions in the previous section, <em><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating Your First Template" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Creating your first template]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></em>.</p><p>The &quot;Use Case View&quot; package contains three element types: (i) Actor, (ii) UseCase, and (iii) Diagram that you can call in a template by typing <strong>&quot;$Actor</strong>,&quot; <strong>&quot;$UseCase</strong>,&quot; and <strong>&quot;$Diagram</strong>&quot; respectively.</p><p>In this example you will create a report that will print the names of the Actors, UseCases, and diagram image.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5863349b-5f1c-4533-8b27-111ca2bfbf72"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1358598953</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="8804a6f4-8982-4de8-b9e7-353a297adadb" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136715055 space=MD2024x version=2 -->
## PAGE 00635: How plugins work

- page_id: `136715055`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715055/How+plugins+work
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-02-13T08:30:34.016+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Developer Guide > Plugins
- labels: ['plugin', 'init']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

On every startup, a modeling tool scans the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Plugins directories' space=''], and searches for subdirectories there:

- If a subdirectory contains the plugin descriptor file, the plugin's manager reads the descriptor file.
- If requirements specified in a descriptor file are fulfilled, the plugin's manager loads a specified class (the specified plugin class must be derived from t he *com.nomagic.magicdraw.plugins.Plugin* class). Then a met hod *init()* of th e loaded class is called. Th e *init()* meth od can add GUI components using the actions architecture or do other activities and return from the method. T he *init()* met hod is called only if isSupported() returns true .

The following figure illustrates how do plugins work.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### PANEL: Related pages

Related pages

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Plugins directories' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE
