<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136711072 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00682: Information Item

- page_id: `136711072`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136711072/Information+Item
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:13:20.626+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Quick search

633879781

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Quick search

633879783

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Quick search

633879782

The [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Information Flow' space='MD2024x'] package provides mechanisms for specifying the exchange of information between entities in a system at a high level of [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Navigating between different levels of abstraction' space='MD2024x']. Information Flows describe the circulation of information in a system in a general manner. They do not specify the nature of the information or the mechanisms by which this information is conveyed (message passing, signal, common data store, parameter of operation, etc.). Also, they do not specify sequences or any control conditions. It is intended that representation and realization [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Link' space='MD2024x'] will be able to specify which model element implements the specified information flow, and how the information will be conveyed while modeling in detail.

An Information Item is an abstraction of all information that can be exchanged between objects. It is a kind of [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying internal structure on structured classifiers' space='MD2024x'] intended to represent information in a very abstract way, one which cannot be instantiated.

In a classifier, the Information Item can be represented as a name inside a rectangle. The black triangle icon at the top of the rectangle indicates that it is an Information Item.

Information Items, or any conveyed classifiers, can be displayed on any relationships.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Example of an Information Item named Wage

One purpose of Information Items is to enable you to define preliminary models before making detailed modeling decisions on types or structures. Another purpose of Information Items and Information Flows is to abstract complex models by using a less specific more general representation of the information exchanged between entities of a system.

To create an Information Item

1. In a model or diagram, select a relationship or create a new one.
2. Set a relationship as an Information Flow, using one of the following methods:
  - Click a relationship. The smart manipulator opens. Select the New Conveyed Information command. [ATTACHMENT filename='smart_manipulator_new_conveyed_information.png']
  - Open the relationship [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024x'] . Select the Conveyed Information property group and click the Add button.
3. The New Conveyed Information dialog opens. Click [ATTACHMENT filename='3dots_button.png'] next to the Conveyed Information text box.
4. The [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Selecting elements' space='MD2024x'] dialog opens. Click the Creation Mode button to enable the creation of new elements.
5. Select the package where you want to create a new element.
6. Click the **Create** button and select **Information Item**element in the list. Quick searchStart typing the element name to find the element quicker.[IMAGE alt='' src='']
7. The Specification window of the newly created element opens. Type the element name and specify other properties according to your needs.
8. After you finish, close all the dialogs one by one. The Information Item element is created.

633879780

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Formatting symbols' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='UML elements' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Information Flow' space='MD2024x']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="075e4f8e-4c00-482f-a590-4d192a4625c9"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">633879781</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a8de90d0-fd1d-40ce-85de-fb395a87da79"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">633879783</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3661a48d-a5e4-49bd-b6b2-15b85f4d8ea6"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">633879782</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>The <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Information Flow" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Information Flows]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> package provides mechanisms for specifying the exchange of information between entities in a system at a high level of <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Navigating between different levels of abstraction" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[abstraction]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. Information Flows describe the circulation of information in a system in a general manner. They do not specify the nature of the information or the mechanisms by which this information is conveyed (message passing, signal, common data store, parameter of operation, etc.). Also, they do not specify sequences or any control conditions. It is intended that representation and realization <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Link" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[links]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> will be able to specify which model element implements the specified information flow, and how the information will be conveyed while modeling in detail.</p><p>An Information Item is an abstraction of all information that can be exchanged between objects. It is a kind of <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Displaying internal structure on structured classifiers" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[classifier]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> intended to represent information in a very abstract way, one which cannot be instantiated.</p><p>In a classifier, the Information Item can be represented as a name inside a rectangle. The black triangle icon at the top of the rectangle indicates that it is an Information Item. </p><p>Information Items, or any conveyed classifiers, can be displayed on any relationships.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="58"><ri:attachment ri:filename="information_item.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Information Item" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Example of an Information Item named Wage</h6><p><span>One purpose of Information Items is to enable you to define preliminary models before making detailed modeling decisions on types or structures. Another purpose of Information Items and Information Flows is to abstract complex models by using a less specific more general representation of the information exchanged between entities of a system.</span></p><p><span><br /></span></p><p><span>To create an Information Item</span></p><hr /><ol><li>In a model or diagram, select a relationship or create a new one.</li><li>Set a relationship as an Information Flow, using one of the following methods:<ul><li>Click a relationship. The smart manipulator opens. Select the <strong>New Conveyed Information</strong> command.<br /><ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="197"><ri:attachment ri:filename="smart_manipulator_new_conveyed_information.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Information Item" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>Open the relationship <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link>. Select the <strong>Conveyed Information </strong>property group and click the <strong>Add</strong> button. </li></ul></li><li>The <strong>New Conveyed Information </strong>dialog opens. Click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="21"><ri:attachment ri:filename="3dots_button.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="UPDM2P185" ri:content-title="_buttons" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> next to the <strong>Conveyed Information </strong>text box.</li><li>The <strong><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Selecting elements" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Select Conveyed Information]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> </strong>dialog opens. Click the Creation Mode button to enable the creation of new elements.</li><li>Select the package where you want to create a new element.</li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Click the <strong>Create</strong> button and select <strong>Information Item </strong>element in the list.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8e069926-c278-4dfe-b6b2-fa4ee690c03c"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Quick search</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Start typing the element name to find the element quicker.</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="create_information_item.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Information Item" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li>The Specification window of the newly created element opens. Type the element name and specify other properties according to your needs.</li><li>After you finish, close all the dialogs one by one. The Information Item element is created.</li></ol></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b2755d1d-1e03-44e1-8455-dd8af9b1c845"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">633879780</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Formatting symbols" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="UML elements" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Model Elements]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Information Flow" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136715275 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00683: Information logging

- page_id: `136715275`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715275/Information+logging
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:17:18.312+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Developer Guide
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Our modeling tools, including MagicDraw, use the Log4j logger. The configuration file is *<program installation directory>/data/log4j2.xml*.

The *<program installation directory>/data/log4j2-test.xml*file is used as a configuration file for program test cases.

The following sample presents the content of the configuration file. It configures the logger to print INFO category messages to a console with a specific pattern.

```xml
]]>
```

The example below displays how information can be logged to the configured log file:

```text

```

More information about Log4j can be found at [https://logging.apache.org/log4j/2.x/](https://logging.apache.org/log4j/2.x/).

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p>Our modeling tools, including MagicDraw, use the Log4j logger. The configuration file is <em>&lt;program installation directory&gt;/data/log4j2.xml</em>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2b68afbe-15b4-4701-a346-6e0620313677"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The <em>&lt;program installation directory&gt;/data/log4j2-test.xml </em>file is used as a configuration file for program test cases.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The following sample presents the content of the configuration file. It configures the logger to print INFO category messages to a console with a specific pattern.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5f71b97e-4d20-4344-85fe-1b929e6019eb"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">xml</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<Configuration status="warn" monitorInterval="30">
    <Appenders>
        <Console name="Console" target="SYSTEM_OUT">
            <PatternLayout pattern="%d [%t] [%X{id} %X{action_name}] %-5p %c - %m%n"/>
        </Console>
    </Appenders>
    <Loggers>
        <Root level="info">
            <AppenderRef ref="Console"/>
        </Root>
    </Loggers>
</Configuration>]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>The example below displays how information can be logged to the configured log file:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9896c4fb-9c75-43c7-ba0b-47da08b95425"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[//used to get logger with category name
org.apache.log4j.Logger logger = org.apache.log4j.Logger.getLogger("GENERAL");

// set thread context values (optional)
ThreadContext.put("id", "My id");
ThreadContext.put("action_name", "My Action");

logger.info("Logging info");
logger.error("Logging error");

//by default the text will be displayed in the form of:
//0000-00-00 00:00:00,000 [THREAD] [My id My Action] INFO  GENERAL - Logging info
//0000-00-00 00:00:00,000 [THREAD] [My id My Action] ERROR GENERAL - Logging error]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>More information about Log4j can be found at <a href="https://logging.apache.org/log4j/2.x/">https://logging.apache.org/log4j/2.x/</a>.</p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p> </p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136709240 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00684: Inheritance tab

- page_id: `136709240`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136709240/Inheritance+tab
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:11:39.035+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > MagicDraw getting started > Understanding the user interface > Model Browser
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1425598369

1425598371

1425598370

INLINE

The Inheritance tab represents classifiers (e.g., packages, data types, and stereotypes) hierarchy within your project. Inheritance according to the UML Specification is shown using the generalization relationship.

If a model element has no generalization relationship, it will not be represented in the Inheritance tab.

To open a new Inheritance tree

1. Open the Containment , Structure , or Inheritance tab.
2. In the open tab toolbar, click [ATTACHMENT filename='open_new_tab_button.png'] and then select Open in New Inheritance Tree .

You can change some Inheritance tab properties in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Customizing environment options' space='MD2024x']**Environment Options** dialog in the **Browser** options group.

The Inheritance tab consists of the following two components:

- Inheritance tab toolbar
- Inheritance tree

##### Inheritance tab toolbar

The Inheritance tab toolbar contains the following buttons

| Button | Tooltip text | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
|  | Collapse All | Collapses all currently expanded elements in the Containment tree. |
|  | Collapse Selected Recursively | Collapses only selected node and all subnodes in that node. |
|  | Open in New Tree | Opens the contents of the selected element in a new tab of the Model Browser. The selected element becomes the root node of the new tree.You can choose to open elements in:New Containment TreeNew Structure TreeNew Inheritance Tree |
|  | Favorites | Opens the Favorites menu for choosing to do one of the following:Add a selected element to favoritesOpen the dialog for managing favorites Navigate to a desired favorite in the Containment tree |
|  | Quick Find | Opens the Quick Find dialog. |
|  | Options | Click to open the Options menu and then select:Invert Tree - inverts the tree hierarchy - shows the childelement at the top of the hierarchy.Show Applied Stereotypes - shows or hides the applied stereotypes next to the element name in the Containment tree. |

##### Working with model elements in the Inheritance tree

You can work with elements in the Inheritance tree using the commands available from the selected element's shortcut menu. The shortcut menu contains the following commands:

| Command | Function |
| --- | --- |
| Select in Containment Tree | Opens the Containment tab and selects the current element in the Containment tree. |
| Select in Structure Tree | Opens the Structure tab and the structured classifier is selected.This command is available for structured classifiers (SysML). |

1425598368

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Model Browser' space='MD2024x']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136711076 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00685: Initial Node

- page_id: `136711076`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136711076/Initial+Node
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:13:21.072+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

An Initial node is a control node where flow starts when the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Activity' space='MD2024x'] is invoked. An Activity may have more than one Initial node.

You can specify an Initial node by changing its property values in the Initial node[Specification window](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Specification+window). Each property is described in the description area on this window.

You can format Initial Node symbol properties in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Symbol Properties dialog' space='MD2024x'].

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>An Initial node is a control node where flow starts when the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Activity" /></ac:link> is invoked. An Activity may have more than one Initial node.</p><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">You can specify an Initial node by changing its property values in the Initial node </span><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024x/Specification+window">Specification window</a><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">. Each property is described in the description area on this window.</span></p><p>You can format Initial Node symbol properties in the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Symbol Properties dialog" /></ac:link>.    </p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="initial_node.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Initial Node" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136711319 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00686: Initial state

- page_id: `136711319`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136711319/Initial+state
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:13:32.080+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements > State Machine > Pseudo States
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Every object belongs to a particular state as soon as it is created. So, it is useful to explicitly show that particular state. A solid filled circle represents the initial state of an object. There can only be one initial state for an object. The initial state denotes the starting place for a transition, the target of which is a composite state.

#### PANEL: Related Pages

Related Pages

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='UML elements' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024x']

#### PANEL: Related pages

Related pages

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Pseudo States' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p>Every object belongs to a particular state as soon as it is created. So, it is useful to explicitly show that particular state. A solid filled circle represents the initial state of an object. There can only be one initial state for an object. The initial state denotes the starting place for a transition, the target of which is a composite state.</p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="36e20852-22dd-420b-8d4b-52b136b22981"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related Pages</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="UML elements" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Model Elements]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Specification window" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Specification Window]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f01b84d9-d9d1-4049-8315-15a6a05d850d"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related pages</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="a91c5c61-63c8-4aaa-bfa5-b56b64a56477"><ac:parameter ac:name="page"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Pseudo States" /></ac:link></ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136714978 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00687: Initializing a Custom Model

- page_id: `136714978`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136714978/Initializing+a+Custom+Model
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:16:59.236+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > UML Profiling and DSL Guide > Domain Specific Language customization > Using Customization Data
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

With DSL customization, you can modify a model element to act according to your needs depending on the stereotype you apply.

###### Default values

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Tag' space='MD2024x'] have default values. Use the default values when you create the tag, making sure its definition has multiplicity greater than zero. In this case it is automatically created when applying a stereotype.

###### Stereotypes on relationship ends

At times, DSL requires applying [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Stereotype' space='MD2024x']to some elements after DSL relationship connection to those elements.

Use the following properties for this purpose:

| Property | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Apply to Source | Specifies stereotypes that must be applied to the source element of this relationship after connection. |
| Apply to Target | Specifies stereotypes that must be applied to the target element of this relationship after connection. |

#### INFO: Source and Target in an example

Source and Target in an example

“Source” and “Target” are respectively the “source” and “target” of a directed relationship.

The first connected element acts as a“source” for a non-directed relationship.

Example:

1. Create a stereotype «serve» and select the Dependency metaclass.
2. Create two stereotypes «main» and «servant» and select the Element metaclass.
3. Create a customization element.
4. In the customization [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024x'] , specify the following property values:
  1. for the Customization Target property, select the «serve» stereotype,
  2. for the Apply To Source property, select the «main» stereotype,
  3. for the Apply To Target property, select the «servant» stereotype.
5. Reopen the project. Draw the «serve» dependency from one element to another. The «main» and «servant» stereotypes are applied to these elements.

###### Required Generalization or Interface Realization

Sometimes DSL requires elements to be subtypes of a general abstract [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class' space='MD2024x']or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Interface' space='MD2024x']. Use the **Super Types**property for this purpose. The **Super Types** property specifies types to be super types of the DSL element. The*Generalization*or*InterfaceRealization*(if using an interface) will be created in the model after applying the customized stereotype.

#### INFO: Example

Example

The following is an example of customization. Every JAVA class will be a subclass of the *Object*class. In other words, every Class marked with the «JAVAClass» stereotype is inherited from the Object.

1. Create a customization element.
2. In the customization Specification window, specify the **Customization Target**property as the «JAVAClass» stereotype.
3. In the customization Specification window, specify the **Super Types**property as the *Object* class.

#### PANEL: On this page

On this page

44

#### PANEL: Related Pages

Related Pages

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating Customization Data' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Using Customization Data' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Model' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Tag' space='MD2024x']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136714833 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00688: Initializing opposite association end

- page_id: `136714833`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136714833/Initializing+opposite+association+end
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:16:51.477+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > UML Profiling and DSL Guide > Working with Profiles > Creating Profiles
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

If two stereotypes are connected with an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Association' space='MD2024x']and there are roles at both ends, the role at one end will be automatically initialized when the opposite role is specified.

#### INFO: Example:

Example:

When «dog» Rex is set as a pet for «human» Tom, Tom will be automatically set as “main” for Rex:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### PANEL: Related pages

Related pages

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Stereotype' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Association' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Association End' space='MD2024x']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136710656 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00689: Inserting a shape on the path

- page_id: `136710656`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136710656/Inserting+a+shape+on+the+path
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:12:50.746+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagramming > Working with paths and relationships
- labels: ['split-transition', 'insert-shape-on-path', 'split-path']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This functionality is available in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='State Machine diagram' space='MD2024x']StateMachine and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Activity diagram' space='MD2024x'] diagrams.

In the State and Activity diagrams, you can split a path into two paths by dropping a shape on it. This is valid for Transition/ Control Flow/ Object Flow relationships and allows you to connect the element shape with the elements connected via the path. 
 
To insert a new shape by splitting the path on the diagram pane

1. Select the shape you want to insert or create a new one.
2. Drag the shape on the path (the path is highlighted in blue) and drop it.
3. In the **Split Transition** dialog, click the appropriate button according to where you want to insert the shape on the path:

- **Before Transition**button. The shape is inserted before the path. It means that a new path is created, then the dropped shape is drawn and then the existing path is drawn. For example, *Password read* transition is drawn from *Read Name* State to*Verification* State. If you want to insert the*Read password* State before the *Password read* Transition, drop the *Read password* State on the Transition, and in the **Split Transition** dialog, choose the **Before Transition** button.[IMAGE alt='' src=''][IMAGE alt='' src='']
- **After Transition**button. The shape is inserted before the path. It means the existing path is created, the dropped element symbol is drawn, and then a new path is drawn. For example, *Name**read* Transition is drawn from *Read Name* State to *Verification* State. If you want to insert the *Read**password* State after the *Name read* Transition, drop the *Read password* State on the Transition, and in the **Split Transition** dialog, choose the **After Transition** button.[IMAGE alt='' src=''][IMAGE alt='' src='']
- **Do Not Insert**button. Action is canceled and the dialog is closed.

In the **Split Transition** dialog, select the **Remember my choice** check box and the next time the shape will be inserted before or after the path, depending on your selection.

****

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="be0eba18-1df8-4cbc-aa4e-4458ff1a6bfb"><ac:rich-text-body><p>This functionality is available in the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="State Machine diagram" /><ac:link-body><span class="confluence-link">State </span>Machine</ac:link-body></ac:link> and <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Activity diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Activity]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> diagrams.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>In the State and Activity diagrams, you can split a path into two paths by dropping a shape on it. This is valid for Transition/ Control Flow/ Object Flow relationships and allows you to connect the element shape with the elements connected via the path. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" />To insert a new shape by splitting the path on the diagram pane</p><hr /><ol><li><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Select the shape you want to insert or create a new one.</span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"> Drag the shape on the path (the path is highlighted in blue) and drop it.<br /></span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"> In the <strong>Split Transition</strong> dialog, click the appropriate button according to where you want to insert the shape on the path:</span><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"> </span></span></li></ol><ul><li><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"> </span><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><strong>Before Transition </strong>button. The shape is inserted before the path. It means that a new path is created, then the dropped shape is drawn and then the existing path is drawn.</span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b22b0b0c-fd35-4399-84a2-38e5a213de0b"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">For example, <em>Password read</em> transition is drawn from <em>Read Name</em> State to<em> Verification</em> State. If you want to insert the<em> Read password</em> State before the <em>Password read</em> Transition, drop the <em>Read password</em> State on the Transition, and in the <span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><strong>Split Transition</strong></span> dialog, choose the <strong>Before Transition</strong> button.</span></span></p><p><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:title="Inserting a shape on the path" ac:alt="Inserting a shape on the path" ac:height="200" ac:width="160"><ri:attachment ri:filename="state_machine_diagram_2.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Inserting a shape on the path" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><ac:image ac:title="Inserting a shape on the path" ac:alt="Inserting a shape on the path" ac:height="242" ac:width="163"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Insert_symbol_before_path_with_state.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Inserting a shape on the path" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><strong>After <span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">Transition </span></strong><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">button</span>. The shape is inserted before the path. It means the existing path is created, the dropped element symbol is drawn, and then a new path is drawn.<br /></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="125c74a4-a702-4fa7-af1e-ad6533f7bf0f"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">For example, <em>Name </em><em>read</em> Transition is drawn from <em>Read Name</em> State to <em>Verification</em> State. If you want to insert the <em>Read</em><em> password</em> State after the <em>Name read</em> Transition, drop the <em>Read password</em> State on the Transition, and in the <span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><strong>Split Transition</strong></span> dialog, choose the <strong>After Transition</strong> button. </span></p><ac:image ac:title="Inserting a shape on the path" ac:alt="Inserting a shape on the path" ac:height="222" ac:width="159"><ri:attachment ri:filename="state_machine_diagram_1.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Inserting a shape on the path" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><ac:image ac:title="Inserting a shape on the path" ac:alt="Inserting a shape on the path" ac:height="252" ac:width="154"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Insert_symbol_after_path_with_state.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Inserting a shape on the path" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><br /></span></p></li><li><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><strong>Do Not Insert </strong>button. Action is canceled and the dialog is closed.</span></li></ul><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="69da0fcd-52d9-42db-9ab9-6f589edb6390"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">In the <span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><strong>Split Transition</strong></span> dialog</span>, select the <strong>Remember my choice</strong> check box and the next time the shape will be inserted before or after the path, depending on your selection.</span></p><ac:image ac:title="Split Transition dialog" ac:alt="Split Transition dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="split_transition_dialog.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Inserting a shape on the path" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><strong><br /></strong></span></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136711835 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00690: Inserting images into HTML text

- page_id: `136711835`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136711835/Inserting+images+into+HTML+text
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:13:58.302+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Working with model elements > HTML editor
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### EXCERPT: By pasting or dragging rich text with an image

INLINE

To insert an image into HTML text

#### EXPAND: From the Containment tree

From the Containment tree

- When the image is already an AttachedFile, please refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with attached files' space='MD2024x'] .

#### EXPAND: By using the Advanced HTML Editor

By using the Advanced HTML Editor

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Advanced HTML Editor dialog' space='MD2024x'] .
2. On the toolbar, click Insert Image button [ATTACHMENT filename='insert_image_button.png'] , then click one of the following:
  - [ATTACHMENT filename='insert_image_button.png'] From File – to browse the image in your file system or paste the attached image file URL into the Picture source field.
  - [ATTACHMENT filename='insert_attached_image.png'] From Attached File – to open the Select Elements dialog and select the appropriate parent element to which the AttachedFile image should be attached.
  - [IMAGE alt='' src=''] **From Image Library** – to use images from a bundled image collection or your own added sets of images.
3. Click OK twice to close both dialogs.

#### EXPAND: By pasting or dragging the image file from file system

By pasting or dragging the image file from file system

1. Open the image file location, select the file(s) you want to insert, then do one of the following:
  - copy/paste them to the HTML Editor. In the **Paste Special**menu, select **Image**to paste image and attach it to the project or **Image (without attaching)** to paste it only for referencing in the diagram.
  - drag them to the HTML Editor. In the **Select Image Owner**, select the appropriate parent element to which the AttachedFile image should be attached. If you click **Cancel**, the image will be displayed in the diagram, but not attached to the project.
2. Click**OK**to close the dialogs. If you chose to not attach the image, it is displayed in the HTML Editor, but when you delete the file from your file system, it is no longer displayed. That also means other users you want to share the project with won't be able to see the image if they open the project from another device.This option is very useful when the file you want to insert is large and you don't want to increase the project size. This feature works on both the HTML Editor and Advanced HTML Editor.

#### EXPAND: By pasting or dragging an image from a web browser

By pasting or dragging an image from a web browser

1. Do either:
  - copy/paste the image to the HTML Editor. In the Paste Special menu, select Image to paste image and attach it to the project or HTML Text to paste it only for referencing in the diagram.
  - drag the image to the HTML Editor.
2. Click **OK** to close the dialogs. This feature works only on the HTML Editor. This feature works only with Google Chrome and Mozilla Firefox.

#### EXPAND: By pasting or dragging rich text with an image

By pasting or dragging rich text with an image

1. Do either:
  - copy/paste content to the HTML Editor. In the Paste Special menu, select HTML Text to choose whether paste it only for referencing in the diagram or attach it to the project, or select Image to paste and attach it to the project, or select Plain Text to paste only text without an image.
  - drag the image to the HTML Editor. In the Select Image Owner , select the appropriate parent element to which the AttachedFile image should be attached. If you click**Cancel**, the image will be displayed in the diagram, but not attached to the project.
2. Click **OK** to close the dialogs. This feature works only on HTML Editor. Only rich text from MS Office files is supported. If the image from the rich text document is not stored in your file system, you are not asked to choose whether to attach the file or not.

To insert multiple image files

1. Open the image files location, select the files you want to insert, then do either:
  - copy/paste them to the HTML Editor. In the Paste Special menu, select Image to paste images and attach them to the project or Image (without attaching) to paste them only for referencing in the diagram.
  - drag them to the HTML Editor. In the Select Image Owner , select the appropriate parent element to which the AttachedFile images should be attached.
2. Click OK to close the dialogs.

This feature works on both HTML Editor and Advanced HTML Editor.

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136711953 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00691: Inspecting changes in the Change details panel

- page_id: `136711953`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136711953/Inspecting+changes+in+the+Change+details+panel
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:14:02.349+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Tools > Model Merge > Analyzing and managing merge results
- labels: ['change-details', 'merge-result']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1991256206

INLINE

1991256208

INLINE

1991256207

INLINE

The **Change details** panel displays a tree structure reflecting the changes that occurred either on the element selected in the **Merged** **Result** tree/list or on the property selected in the **Specification** panel. The panel is located under the **Specification** panel on the **Merge** window.

The panel contains a tree structure that has two top-level branches – Source changes and Target changes – in 3-way merge, and 1 top-level branch – Source changes – in 2-way merge. In this panel, you can accept or reject changes using the shortcut menu.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

In this panel, you can see dependent changes. In order to see them, expand the **Dependent changes** branch under an element with dependent changes.

For the dependent changes description, refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding change concept' space='MD2024x'].

The Multiplicity attribute is merged as a string, meaning that Lower Value and Upper Value are merged and transformed to numerical/text types accordingly.

1991256204

INLINE

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Getting merge summary information and changes legend' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding content of the Merged Result panel' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Inspecting element property changes in the Specification panel' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying and navigating through changes' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Quickly navigating through conflicting changes' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Accepting and rejecting changes using shortcut menu' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Viewing and analyzing changes of modified diagrams' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Generating a differences report' space='MD2024x']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136712761 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00692: Inspecting differences in the Change details panel

- page_id: `136712761`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136712761/Inspecting+differences+in+the+Change+details+panel
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:14:50.866+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Model analysis > Comparing projects > Analyzing comparison results
- labels: ['change-details', 'differences', 'difference-viewer']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The **Change details** panel displays a tree structure reflecting the differences between the two versions of either the element selected in the **Differences** tree or the property selected in the **Specification** panel. If several elements or properties are selected, then all changes that have been detected in these elements or properties accordingly will be displayed in the **Change** **details** panel. The panel is located above the **Specification** panel on the **Difference Viewer** window.

The panel contains a tree structure that has 1 top-level branch – Changes.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

In this panel, you can see dependent changes. In order to see them, expand the **Dependent changes** branch under an element with dependent changes.

For the dependent changes description, refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding change types' space='MD2024x'].

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>The <strong>Change details</strong> panel displays a tree structure reflecting the differences between the two versions of either the element selected in the <strong>Differences</strong> tree or the property selected in the <strong>Specification</strong> panel. If several elements or properties are selected, then all changes that have been detected in these elements or properties accordingly will be displayed in the <strong>Change</strong> <strong>details</strong> panel. The panel is located above the <strong>Specification</strong> panel on the <strong>Difference Viewer</strong> window.</p><p>The panel contains a tree structure that has 1 top-level branch <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="edfa489e-d703-4e81-84d4-4e561ff6619a">– Changes.</ac:inline-comment-marker>   <span style="color: rgb(129,137,156);"> <br /> </span></p><p><ac:image ac:title="Change details dialog" ac:alt="Change details dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="change_details_dialog.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Inspecting differences in the Change details panel" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>In this panel, you can see dependent changes. In order to see them, expand the <strong>Dependent changes</strong> branch under an element with dependent changes.</p><p>For the dependent changes description, r<ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="319a5311-84d4-4531-8f99-20cc6ed2e97e">efer t</ac:inline-comment-marker>o <ac:link ac:anchor="dependentchange"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Understanding change types" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Dependent changes]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><p> </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136712757 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00693: Inspecting element property changes in the differences Specification panel

- page_id: `136712757`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136712757/Inspecting+element+property+changes+in+the+differences+Specification+panel
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:14:50.630+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Model analysis > Comparing projects > Analyzing comparison results
- labels: ['property-changes', 'compare-specification-panel']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can see all the details of the element property changes in the **Specification** panel that is located at the bottom of the **Difference Viewer** window. All you need to do is selecting the appropriate element in the **Differences** tree.

As you can see in the following figure, the first column of the panel contains a list of element property names, and other columns display their value changes that have been detected in both base and compared projects.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

To navigate from the **Specification** panel to the **Differences** tree, do either

- In the Properties column, right-click the name of a property, which references other elements. Then on its shortcut menu, point to Select in Differences tree and choose an element to which you want to navigate.
- I n the Base or Compared column, right-click the value of a property, which references other elements. Then on its shortcut menu choose an element to which you want to navigate.

[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
 **Inspecting differences between textual values**

You can see exact changes between textual values of element properties, such as element documentation, comments, pre- and post- conditions of a use case. Differences of compared texts can be displayed in a single dialog, where inserted and deleted parts of the text are appropriately highlighted. As of version 18.3, the differences between compared text written in HTML are now represented as rich text, enabling you to see all changes in formatting as well.

[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
 To see the differences of compared texts, do either

- Select a property with a modified text on the Specification panel and then click [ATTACHMENT filename='compare_property_values_button.png'] on the toolbar.
- Double-click the property on the Specification panel. The Compare property dialog opens.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p>You can see all the details of the element property changes in the <strong>Specification</strong> panel that is located at the bottom of the <strong>Difference Viewer</strong> window. All you need to do is selecting the appropriate element in <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="a2033ad1-57fd-4c86-95d8-852c0e08f869">the </ac:inline-comment-marker> <strong> <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="a2033ad1-57fd-4c86-95d8-852c0e08f869">Differences</ac:inline-comment-marker> </strong> <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="a2033ad1-57fd-4c86-95d8-852c0e08f869"> tree.</ac:inline-comment-marker></p><p>As you can see in the following figure, the first column of the panel contains a list of element property names, and other columns display their value changes t<ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="027b7e7d-5dca-4150-815d-e43fed998a3a">hat have been d</ac:inline-comment-marker>etected in both base and compared projects.</p><p><ac:image ac:alt="Displaying element property changes in Specification panel" ac:title="Displaying element property changes in Specification panel"><ri:attachment ri:filename="compare_specification_panel.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Inspecting element property changes in the differences Specification panel" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p> </p><p>To navigate from the <strong>Specification</strong> panel to the <strong>Differences</strong> tree, do either</p><hr /><ul><li>In the <strong>Properties</strong> column, right-click the name of a property, which references other elements. Then on its shortcut menu, point to <strong>Select in Differences </strong>tree<strong> </strong>and choose an element to which you want to navigate.</li><li><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">I</span>n the <strong> Base </strong>or <strong>Compared</strong> column, right-click the value of a property, which references other elements. Then on its shortcut menu choose an element to which you want to navigate.</li></ul><p><ac:image ac:alt="Using property name shortcut menu to navigate from Specification panel to Differences tree" ac:title="Using property name shortcut menu to navigate from Specification panel to Differences tree"><ri:attachment ri:filename="compare_specification_panel_shortcut.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Inspecting element property changes in the differences Specification panel" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> <span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"> <strong>Inspecting differences between textual values</strong> </span></p><p>You can see exact changes between textual values of element properties, such as element documentation, comments, pre- and post- conditions of a use case. Differences of compared texts can be displayed in a single dialog, where inserted and deleted parts of the text are appropriately highlighted. As of version 18.3, the differences between compared text written in HTML are now represented as rich text, enabling you to see all changes in formatting as well.</p><p><ac:image ac:alt="Displaying differences between long textual property values" ac:title="Displaying differences between long textual property values"><ri:attachment ri:filename="compare_property_dialog.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Inspecting element property changes in the differences Specification panel" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="6cb413be-44fc-4f1b-81ae-6764a0ebb8c2">To see the differences of compared texts, do either</ac:inline-comment-marker></p><hr /><ul><li>Select a property with a modified text on the <strong>Specification</strong> panel and then click <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="compare_property_values_button.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="_buttons" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> on the toolbar.</li><li>Double-click the property on the <strong>Specification</strong> panel. The <strong>Compare p<ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="d112cebf-7f31-45f8-a49d-efa7ed5fd504">roperty</ac:inline-comment-marker> </strong> <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="d112cebf-7f31-45f8-a49d-efa7ed5fd504"> dialog opens.</ac:inline-comment-marker></li></ul></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p> </p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136711949 space=MD2024x version=2 -->
## PAGE 00694: Inspecting element property changes in the Specification panel

- page_id: `136711949`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136711949/Inspecting+element+property+changes+in+the+Specification+panel
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-01-30T14:55:03.677+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Tools > Model Merge > Analyzing and managing merge results
- labels: ['merge-specification-panel', 'property-changes']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1086956613

1086956615

1086956614

You can see all the details of the element property changes in the **Specification** panel that is located at the top right of the **Merge** window. All you need to do is selecting the element in the **Merged Result** tree or list.

As you can see in the following figure, the first column of the panel contains a list of element property names, and other columns display their value changes that have been detected in both source and the and target. Third column Merge Results Preview shows the final merge result. Properties changes can be accepted or rejected in this panel.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

To navigate from the **Specification** panel to the **Merged Result** tree, do either

- In the Properties column, right-click the name of a property, which references other elements. Then on its shortcut menu, point to Select in Merged Result Tree and choose an element to which you want to navigate.
- In the Source , Ancestor , Target or Merge Result Preview column, right-click the value of a property, which references other elements. Then on its shortcut menu choose an element to which you want to navigate.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

The **Ancestor** column by default is hidden. To show it in the **Specification**panel, click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] on the **Merge**toolbar and select **Show Ancestor**.

The Multiplicity attribute is merged as a string, meaning that Lower Value and Upper Value are merged and transformed into numerical/text types accordingly.

**Inspecting differences between textual values**

You can see exact changes between textual values of element properties, such as element documentation, comments, pre- and post-conditions of a use case. Differences of compared texts can be displayed in a single dialog, where inserted and deleted parts of the text are highlighted.

[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
To see the differences of compared texts, do either

- Select a property with a modified text on the Specification panel and then click [ATTACHMENT filename='compare_property_values_button.png'] on the toolbar.
- Double-click the property on the Specification panel. The Compare property dialog opens.

1086956612

**Related pages**

**[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Model Merge' space='']**

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136714064 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00695: Installation

- page_id: `136714064`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136714064/Installation
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:16:05.874+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard > Report Wizard template editor
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

To install Report Wizard Template Editor in Microsoft Word

1. Copy all files and subdirectories from the <install.dir>\plugins\com.nomagic.magicdraw.reportwizard\vba folder to %APPDATA%\Microsoft\Word\STARTUP**** (%APPDATA% is the location where user data is stored). For example,
  - On Windows 2000 and Windows XP: C:\Documents and Settings\User Name\Application Data\Microsoft\Word\STARTUP
  - On Windows Vista: C:\Users\UserName\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Word\STARTUP
  - On Windows 7, 8, and later: C:\Users\UserName\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Word\STARTUP
2. Restart Microsoft Word.

Microsoft Word 64bit may require mscomctl.ocx for this add-in. To install mscomctl.ocx:

1. Go to [https://www.microsoft.com/en-au/download/details.aspx?id=10019](https://www.microsoft.com/en-au/download/details.aspx?id=10019)

2. Download Microsoft Visual Basic 6.0 Common Controls

3. Install mscomctl.ocx by extracting VisualBasic6-????.exe and then copy mscomctl.ocx to C:\Windows\System32 or C:\Windows\SysWOW64

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>To install Report Wizard Template Editor in Microsoft Word</p><hr /><ol><li>Copy all files and subdirectories from the <em>&lt;install.dir&gt;\plugins\com.nomagic.magicdraw.reportwizard\vba</em> folder to <em>%APPDATA%\Microsoft\Word\STARTUP<strong> </strong></em>(%APPDATA% is the location where user data is stored). For example,<br /><ul><li>On Windows 2000 and Windows XP: <em>C:\Documents and Settings\User Name\Application Data\Microsoft\Word\STARTUP</em></li><li>On Windows Vista: <em>C:\Users\UserName\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Word\STARTUP</em></li><li>On Windows 7, 8, and later: <em>C:\Users\UserName\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Word\STARTUP</em></li></ul></li><li>Restart Microsoft Word.<br /><br /></li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="edbea50e-de80-4591-a358-d537c22b71e9"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Microsoft Word 64bit may require mscomctl.ocx for this add-in. To install mscomctl.ocx:</p><p>1. Go to <a href="https://www.microsoft.com/en-au/download/details.aspx?id=10019">https://www.microsoft.com/en-au/download/details.aspx?id=10019</a></p><p>2. Download Microsoft Visual Basic 6.0 Common Controls</p><p>3. Install mscomctl.ocx by extracting VisualBasic6-????.exe and then copy mscomctl.ocx to C:\Windows\System32 or C:\Windows\SysWOW64</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

## PAGE 00697: Installing custom tool

- page_id: `136714610`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136714610/Installing+custom+tool
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:16:43.319+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard > Appendix A: report extensions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

To install the extension, copy the *JAR* file containing a custom tool class into the extensions directory under the Report Wizard plugin directory or template directory. Report Wizard will automatically load a new *JAR* file when a new report is generated.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>To install the extension, copy the <em>JAR</em> file containing a custom tool class into the extensions directory under the Report Wizard plugin directory or template directory. Report Wizard will automatically load a new <em>JAR</em> file when a new report is generated.</p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136711078 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00698: Instance Specification

- page_id: `136711078`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136711078/Instance+Specification
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:13:21.448+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements
- labels: ['slot', 'slot-specification']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

72524162

72524165

116896173

**On this page**

4

72524164

INLINE

##### Introduction

An instance specification specifies the existence of an entity in a modeled system and completely or partiallydescribes the entity.

The description may include:

- The classification of an entity by one or more classifiers of which the entity is an instance. If the only classifier specified is abstract, then the instance specification only partially describes the entity.
- A kind of instance based on its classifier or classifiers. For example, an instance specification whose classifier is a class describes an object of that class, while an instance specification whose classifier is an association describes a link of that association.
- A specification of structural features values of the entity. Not all structural features of all classifiers of the instance specification need to be represented by slots, in which case the instance specification is a partial description.
- A specification of how to compute, derive, or construct the instance (optional).

MagicDraw allows you to create the instances of classifiers such as [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class' space='MD2024x'], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Interface' space='MD2024x'], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Enumeration' space='MD2024x'], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Use Case' space='MD2024x'], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Actor' space='MD2024x'],[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Node' space='MD2024x'], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Component' space='MD2024x'], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Manifestations' space='MD2024x'].

In the Component (or Deployment) diagram Node Instance, Component Instance, Artifact Instance elements are the same Instance Specification elements with an assigned component, node or artifact.

The instances are shown using a rectangle by underlining the name string of an instance element. The instance of an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Actor' space='MD2024x']is shown as an actor “stick man” figure with the actor’s name below the symbol.

You can specify instance properties in the instance [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024x']. In the same window, you can find a description of each property. Descriptions are presented in the description area of the Specification window. If you need to handle a huge amount of instance specifications, use the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Instance table' space='MD2024x'].

##### Assigning a classifier to an Instance Specification

To assign a classifier to an instance, do one of the following

- On the diagram, click an instance shape, and then on the smart manipulator toolbar, click [IMAGE alt='' src='']. In the open list, select the classifier.
- On the diagram, click an instance shape, and then press **Ctrl+T**. In the open list, select the classifier.
- On the diagram, click an instance shape, then click the name area. Type the “:” and then type the classifier name. Press **Enter**or click anywhere on the diagram. The classifier is created and assigned. Type the “:” colon, then press Ctrl+Space or Ctrl+Backspace and then in the open list, select the classifier.
- In the Containment tree, select a classifier and drag it to the instance shape.

Use the Node Instance button to create the Instance Specification withassigned Node and the Node Instance will have a Node shape.

##### Assigning Instance Specification as Default Value

To assign Instance Specification as Default Value

- Drag Instance Specification element on Classifier property on a diagram.

Drag-and-drop feature is available only if Instance Specification classifiers are compatible with the Property type and ifProperty is editable.

Setting the initial value to an attribute of the assigned classifier

To set the initial value to an attribute of the assigned classifier

1. Open the Instance Specification window.
2. In the **Slots**property group, select a property and c lick the **Create Value**button.
3. Type the needed value in the field on the right.

##### Showing slots of the assigned classifier

To show slots of the assigned classifier

1. Select an instance shape and click the Compartments button [IMAGE alt='' src=''].
2. In the menu, select **Slots**.

By default slots of the classifier are suppressed.

##### Displaying slot type on the Instance Specification symbol

To display slot type on the instance symbol

1. Open instance **[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Symbol Properties dialog' space='MD2024x']** dialog.
2. Set the **Show Slot Type**property to one of the following:

- None
- Name
- Qualified name

##### Displaying specification value on the Instance Specification symbol

To display specification value on the Instance Specification symbol

1. Open instance [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Symbol Properties dialog' space='MD2024x'] dialog.
2. Select the **Show Specification Value** check box.

##### Saving Instance values to the default property values of the Class (Block)

When you have a Class (Block) and an Instance of that Class (Block)which contains some particular values, you can save those values as default to the Values property of the Class (Block).

To save the Instance values as default

1. Do one of the following:
  - On the diagram pane, select an Instance.
  - In the Containment tree, select an Instance.
  - In the Instance table, select an Instance.
2. Right-click an Instance and from the shortcut menu, select Tools > Save to Default Value(s) .
3. In the open dialog, select the needed options:
  - **Save to composite parts recursively**.
  - **Redefine inherited properties**.
  - Save derived property values . [ATTACHMENT filename='save_to_default_values.png']
4. Click OK .

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### An example of Instance property values saved as default in the Block value properties. The "Save to composite parts recursively" option was selected.

72524160

the instances of classifiers such as <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Class" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[class]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Interface" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[interface]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Enumeration" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[enumeration]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Use Case" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[use case]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Actor" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[actor]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, </span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Node" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[node]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Component" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[component]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Manifestations" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[artifact]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">In the Component (or Deployment) diagram Node Instance, Component Instance, Artifact Instance elements are the same Instance Specification elements with an assigned component, node or artifact.</span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">The instances are shown using a rectangle by underlining the name string of an instance element. The instance of an <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Actor" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[actor ]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>is shown as an actor “stick man” figure with the actor’s name below the symbol.</span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><span style="color: rgb(124,133,153);"><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><span>You can specify instance properties in the instance <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link>. In the same window, you can find a description of each property. Descriptions are presented in the description area of the Specification window. <span>If you need to handle a huge amount of instance specifications, use the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Instance table" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[instance table]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</span></span></span></span></span></p><h3><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">Assigning a classifier to an Instance Specification</span><br /></span></h3><p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">To assign a classifier to an instance, do one of the following</span></p><hr /><ul><li><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><span style="color: rgb(124,133,153);"><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><span><span style="color: rgb(124,133,153);"><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">On the diagram, click an instance shape, and then on the smart manipulator toolbar, click <ac:image ac:title="Specify classifier" ac:alt="Specify classifier"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specify_classifier.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Instance Specification" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>. In the open list, select the classifier.</span></span></span></span></span></span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><span style="color: rgb(124,133,153);"><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><span><span style="color: rgb(124,133,153);"><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">On the diagram, click an instance shape, and then press <strong>Ctrl+T</strong>. In the open list, select the classifier.</span></span></span></span></span></span></li><li><p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><span><span style="color: rgb(124,133,153);"><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><span><span style="color: rgb(124,133,153);"><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">On the diagram, click an instance shape, then click the name area. Type the “:” and then type the classifier name. Press <strong>Enter </strong>or click anywhere on the diagram. The classifier is created and assigned.<br /></span></span></span></span></span></span></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6d7e8dfb-0b4c-4ecc-893c-1600df9969f9"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Type the “:” colon, then press Ctrl+Space or Ctrl+Backspace and then in the open list, select the classifier.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"> </span></p></li><li><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><span style="color: rgb(124,133,153);"><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><span><span style="color: rgb(124,133,153);"><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">In the Containment tree, select a classifier and drag it to the instance shape.</span></span></span></span></span></span></li></ul><p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">Use the Node Instance button to create the Instance Specification with </span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">assigned Node and the Node Instance will have a Node shape.</span></p><h3><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">Assigning Instance Specification as Default Value</span><br /></span></h3><p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">To assign Instance Specification as Default Value</span></p><hr /><ul><li><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><span style="color: rgb(124,133,153);"><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><span>Drag Instance Specification element on Classifier property on a diagram.</span></span></span></span></li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="59526042-f1f1-4b89-a141-3b6119830bea"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">Drag-and-drop feature is available only if Instance Specification classifiers are compatible with the Property type and if </span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">Property is editable.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);font-size: 16.0px;font-weight: bold;letter-spacing: -0.006em;"><br /></span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);font-size: 16.0px;font-weight: bold;letter-spacing: -0.006em;">S</span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);font-size: 16.0px;font-weight: bold;letter-spacing: -0.006em;">etting the initial value to an attribute of the assigned classifier</span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><br /> </span>To set the initial value to an attribute of the assigned classifier</p><hr /><ol><li><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">Open the Instance Specification window.</span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">In the <strong>Slots </strong>property group, select a property and c</span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">lick the </span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><strong>Create Value </strong>button.</span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">Type the needed value in the field on the right.</span></li></ol><h3><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">Showing slots of the assigned classifier</span><br /></span></h3><p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">To show slots of the assigned classifier</span></p><hr /><ol><li><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">Select an instance shape and click the Compartments button <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="compartments.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Instance Specification" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>.</span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">In the menu, select <strong>Slots</strong>.</span></li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="dcc298f1-10a6-4be1-8e08-8f174e553f7f"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">By default slots of the classifier are suppressed.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">Displaying slot type on the Instance Specification symbol</span></h3><p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">To display slot type on the instance symbol</span></p><hr /><ol><li><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">Open instance <strong><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Symbol Properties dialog" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Symbol Properties]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></strong> dialog.</span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">Set the </span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><strong>Show Slot Type </strong>property to one of the following:</span></li></ol><ul><li><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">None</span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">Name</span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">Qualified name</span></li></ul><h3><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><span style="color: rgb(199,200,202);"><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><span style="color: rgb(168,169,173);"><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">Displaying specification value on the Instance Specification symbol</span><br /></span></span></span></span></span></span></h3><p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">To display specification value on the Instance Specification symbol</span></p><hr /><ol><li><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">Open instance </span><strong><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Symbol Properties dialog" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Symbol Properties]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></strong><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"> dialog.</span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">Select the <strong>Show Specification Value</strong> check box.</span></li></ol><h3><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">Saving Instance values to the default property values of the Class (Block)</span></h3><p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">When you have a <span>Class (Block)</span> and an Instance of that <span>Class (Block) </span>which contains some particular values, you can save those values as default to the Values property of the Class (Block).</span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><br /></span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">To save the Instance values as default</span></p><hr /><ol><li>Do one of the following:<ul><li>On the diagram pane, select an Instance.</li><li>In the Containment tree, select an Instance.</li><li>In the Instance table, select an Instance.</li></ul></li><li>Right-click an Instance and from the shortcut menu, select <strong>Tools</strong> &gt;<strong> Save to Default Value(s)</strong>.</li><li>In the open dialog, select the needed options:<ul><li><p><strong>Save to composite parts recursively</strong>.</p></li><li><p><strong>Redefine inherited properties</strong>.</p></li><li><strong>Save derived property values</strong>.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="save_to_default_values.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Instance Specification" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ul></li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong>.</li></ol><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="save_to_default_values_example.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Instance Specification" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">An example of Instance property values saved as default in the Block value properties. The &quot;Save to composite parts recursively&quot; option was selected.</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><br /></span></p><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="487cc943-1a6b-47f9-985e-b35d790c625a"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">72524160</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="UML elements" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Model Elements]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Class" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Use Case" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Use Case diagram" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Sequence diagram" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Activity diagram" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Class diagram" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

## PAGE 00699: Instance table

- page_id: `136710347`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136710347/Instance+table
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-01-30T14:46:15.633+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagrams > Tables
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

221798943

221798946

221798941

**On this page**

221798945

##### **Description**

This feature is available in Standard, Professional, Architect, and Enterprise editions.

The Instance Table is one of four MagicDraw tables which allow you to manage Instance Specifications of your model in a spreadsheet-like form. Each row in the table represents an instance.****

##### **Purpose**

An Instance Table allows you to:

- Create Instance Specifications (instances) for the classifiers.
- Review the instances of one or more of the classifiers in a single place.
- Edit Slot values of the instances displayed in the table.
- Customize the representation of the table.
- Export the data into .html , .csv, or * .xlsx files.

##### **Example**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### **Specific tasks in the Instance Table**

The following outlines the specific procedures when working with the Instance Table:

221837881
