<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136711086 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00701: Interaction

- page_id: `136711086`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136711086/Interaction
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:13:22.317+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

An Interaction is a unit of [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Behavior' space='MD2024x'] focusing on the observable exchange of information between connectable elements, a specialization of Interaction Fragment and of Behavior.

An Interaction originates in a modeling tool while creatingCommunicationandSequencediagrams. When you double click on an Interaction, a diagram is opened. 
In theelementdialog box, you can modify the current element and add various specifications to it. For more information about element dialog boxes and panes of the element dialog boxes,click here. 
 
To open the**Interaction**dialog box, choose the**Specification**command from the Interaction shortcut menu. 
 
In the**Interaction**dialog box, the**Messages**pane lists Messages contained in this Interaction.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>An Interaction is a unit of <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Behavior" /></ac:link> focusing on the observable exchange of information between connectable elements, a specialization of Interaction Fragment and of Behavior.</p><p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">An Interaction originates in a modeling tool while creating </span><a>Communication</a><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"> and </span><a>Sequence</a><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"> diagrams. When you double click on an Interaction, a diagram is opened. </span><br /><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">In the </span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">element</span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"> dialog box, you can modify the current element and add various specifications to it. For more information about element dialog boxes and panes of the element dialog boxes, </span><a>click here</a><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">. </span><br /><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">  </span><br /><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">To open the </span><span class="hcp1" style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><strong>Interaction</strong></span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"> dialog box, choose the </span><strong>Specification</strong><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"> command from the Interaction shortcut menu.   </span><br /><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">  </span><br /><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">In the </span><span class="hcp1" style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><strong>Interaction</strong></span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"> dialog box, the </span><span class="hcp1" style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><strong>Messages</strong></span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"> pane lists Messages contained in this Interaction.</span></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136711087 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00702: Interaction operand

- page_id: `136711087`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136711087/Interaction+operand
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:13:22.686+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements > Interaction
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

An Interaction operand represents an Interaction with an optional guard expression. The Interaction operand is executed only if the guard expression tests true at runtime. The object that starts the interaction requires all the information necessary to make the decision about whether to proceed. The absence of any guard condition means that the interaction is always executed. 
In the Interaction operand [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024x'], you can modify the current element and add various specifications to it.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">An Interaction operand represents an Interaction with an optional guard expression. The Interaction operand is executed only if the guard expression tests true at runtime. The object that starts the interaction requires all the information necessary to make the decision about whether to proceed. The absence of any guard condition means that the interaction is always executed. </span><br /><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">In the <span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">Interaction operand <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link></span></span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">, you can modify the current element and add various specifications to it. </span><br /><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">  </span></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136709840 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00703: Interaction Overview diagram

- page_id: `136709840`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136709840/Interaction+Overview+diagram
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:12:10.720+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagrams > UML diagrams
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

308856586

308856588

308856587

**Overview**

An Interaction Overview diagram is a type of Interaction diagram, which falls under the behavioral diagramming family.An Interaction Overview diagram focuses on the flow of control between interactions. It is based on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Activity diagram' space='MD2024x'] notation. Interactions in the Interaction Overview diagram are represented using the[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Interaction Use' space='MD2024x'] element. All elements in the diagram, except for the Interaction Use, are the same as in the Activity diagram.

**Purpose**

An Interaction Overview diagram is intended to be used to model the flow of logic in a series of interactions. To put it another way, the Interaction Overview diagram uses blocks of interactions and individual interactions instead of modeling a series of discrete behaviors (activities).

**Usage**

Interaction Overview diagrams visualize a sequence of activities just like Activity diagrams do; however, they picture each individual entry as a frame which can contain a nested interaction diagram.

**Summary**

Interaction Overview diagrams are valuable because they:

- provide a high-level view of the logical progression through a set of interactions
- keep the focus on the "big picture" and away from implementation details
- encourage the reuse of interactions

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Example of an Interaction Overview diagram

308856585

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating diagrams' space='MD2024x']

It is based on the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Activity diagram" /></ac:link> notation. Interactions in the Interaction Overview diagram are represented using the<ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Interaction Use" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[ Interaction Use]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> element. All elements in the diagram, except for the Interaction Use, are the same as in the Activity diagram.</p><p><strong>Purpose</strong></p><p>An Interaction Overview diagram is intended to be used to model the flow of logic in a series of interactions. To put it another way, the Interaction Overview diagram uses blocks of interactions and individual interactions instead of modeling a series of discrete behaviors (activities). </p><p><strong>Usage</strong></p><p>Interaction Overview diagrams visualize a sequence of activities just like Activity diagrams do; however, they picture each individual entry as a frame which can contain a nested interaction diagram. </p><p><strong>Summary</strong></p><p>Interaction Overview diagrams are valuable because they:</p><ul><li>provide a high-level view of the logical progression through a set of interactions</li><li>keep the focus on the &quot;big picture&quot; and away from implementation details</li><li>encourage the reuse of interactions </li></ul><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="interaction_overview_diagram_example.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Interaction Overview diagram" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Example of an Interaction Overview diagram<br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br /></h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e2a23b37-52a4-4422-9417-7709aa16f0f8"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">308856585</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Creating diagrams" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136709482 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00704: Interaction Overview diagram elements

- page_id: `136709482`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136709482/Interaction+Overview+diagram+elements
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:11:50.720+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Working with projects > Importing data from other UML tools and formats > Enterprise Architect Import > Special Transformation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

560251696

560251698

560251694

**On this page**

3

560251697

The following sections describe additional EA specific Interaction Overview diagram information.

##### Interaction Overview Diagram

An [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Interaction Overview diagram' space='MD2024x'] is one of four types of Interaction diagrams. The other three are [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Time diagram' space='MD2024x'], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Sequence diagram' space='MD2024x'], and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Communication diagram' space='MD2024x']diagrams. An Interaction Overview diagram, just like an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Activity diagram' space='MD2024x'], visualizes a sequence of activities. Most of the notation elements for Interaction Overview diagrams are the same as those for Activity diagrams (such as Initial, Decision, Fork, Join, and Final [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Node' space='MD2024x']). Interaction Occurrences and Interaction elements are two new elements in the interaction overview diagrams.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Interaction Overview Diagram.

Afterconversion, the Interaction Overview diagram will be placed within anActivityelement.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Interaction Overview Diagram Placement.

###### Interaction Element

Interaction elements display an inline Interaction diagram (Interaction Overview, Timing, Sequence, or Communication diagram). In EA, Interaction elements can be created to display other diagrams than those classified as Interaction diagrams.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Interaction element.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Interaction Element in Interaction Overview Diagram.

###### Interaction Occurrence

Interaction Occurrence elements refer to an existing Interaction diagram. They are visually represented by a rectangular frame, which can be created by dragging the Interaction diagram from the project Browser to an Interaction Overview diagram and selecting the **Interaction Occurrence** command from the shortcut menu.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Interaction Occurence element.

560258099

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Interaction Overview diagram' space='MD2024x']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136711088 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00705: Interaction Use

- page_id: `136711088`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136711088/Interaction+Use
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:13:22.949+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements > Interaction
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Interactions are units of behavior of an enclosing Classifier. They focus on the passing of information with Messages between the Connectable Elements of the Classifier.

A reference to the interaction can be created.

You can [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Editing property values' space='MD2024x'] in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024x']. In the same window, you can find the description of each property. Descriptions are presented in the description area of the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024x']Specification window.

To add a reference to an interaction use, do one of the following

- Open the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024x'] of the interaction use and click the cell of the **Refers To**property value. Then click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] and in the open dialog, select the element. Click **OK**when you are done.
- On the diagram, right-click the interaction use shape, and then point to **Refers To**. Then select an element you wish to refere to.
- On the diagram, click an interaction use shape, and then press **Ctrl+T**. Then select an element you wish to refere to.
- On the diagram, click an interaction use shape, and then on the smart manipulator toolbar, click [IMAGE alt='' src='']. Then select an element you wish to refere to.
- In the Containment tree, select an element and drag it to the interaction use shape.

To add an actual [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Gate' space='MD2024x']

1. Add a reference to the diagram, from which frame the formal message is created.
2. Draw an actual message with the selected formal [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Gate' space='MD2024x'] to the current interaction use.

The rake icon[IMAGE alt='' src='']is displayed on the shape if the interaction use refers to another sequence diagram. The rake icon is not shown by default therefore you need to set the[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying rake icon' space='MD2024x']property value to true in the[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Symbol Properties dialog' space='MD2024x']dialog.

#### PANEL: Related Pages

Related Pages

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='UML elements' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class diagram' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Use Case' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Use Case diagram' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Sequence diagram' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Activity diagram' space='MD2024x']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136711091 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00706: Interface

- page_id: `136711091`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136711091/Interface
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:13:23.429+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

An interface is a specifier for the externally-visible operations of a class, component, or other classifiers (including subsystems) without a specification of the internal structure. Each interface often specifies only a limited part of the behavior of an actual class.

The set of interfaces realized by a classifier is its provided interfaces, which represent the obligations that instances of that classifier have to their clients. They describe the services that the instances of that classifier offer to their clients.

You can [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Editing property values' space='MD2024x'] in the interface [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024x']. In the same window, you can find the description of each property. Descriptions are presented in the description area of the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024x'].

#### PANEL: Related pages

Related pages

true

#### PANEL: Related Pages

Related Pages

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='UML elements' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class diagram' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Use Case' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Use Case diagram' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Sequence diagram' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Activity diagram' space='MD2024x']Activity diagram

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p>An interface is a specifier for the externally-visible operations of a class, component, or other classifiers (including subsystems) without a specification of the internal structure. Each interface often specifies only a limited part of the behavior of an actual class.</p><p>The set of interfaces realized by a classifier is its provided interfaces, which represent the obligations that instances of that classifier have to their clients. They describe the services that the instances of that classifier offer to their clients.</p><p>You can <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Editing property values" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[specify interface properties]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> in the interface <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link>. In the same window, you can find the description of each property. Descriptions are presented in the description area of the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link>.</p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d32cf623-2920-40bb-a45b-fe7b1779bc81"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related pages</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="9b681f01-b5a5-4167-86c3-d99aa4adbde2"><ac:parameter ac:name="all">true</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p> </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1b2025c5-70c1-47b6-9a1b-4fb242548002"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related Pages</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="UML elements" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Model Elements]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Class diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Class diagrams]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Use Case" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Use Case diagram" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Sequence diagram" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Activity diagram" /><ac:link-body>Activity diagram<br /></ac:link-body></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_equal"><ac:layout-cell><p> </p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p> </p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136711100 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00707: Interface realization

- page_id: `136711100`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136711100/Interface+realization
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:13:24.758+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

An Interface realization is a specialized [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Realization' space='MD2024x'] relationship between a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Classifier Behavior property' space='CST190'] and an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Interface' space='MD2024x'], signifying that the realizing classifier conforms to the contract specified by the Interface.

A classifier that implements an interface specifies instances conforming to the interface and its ancestors. A classifier may implement a number of interfaces.

In the Interface realization [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024x'], you can modify the current element and add various specifications to it. 
 
To open theInterfaceRealizationdialog box, choose theSpecificationcommand from the Realization shortcut menu. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

In the Interface Realization [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024x'], the Implementing Classifier property references the classifier that realizes the Interface to which it points.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>An Interface realization is a specialized <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Realization" /></ac:link> relationship between a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="CST190" ri:content-title="Classifier Behavior property" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Classifier Behavior]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> and an <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Interface" /></ac:link>, signifying that the realizing classifier conforms to the contract specified by the Interface.</p><p>A classifier that implements an interface specifies instances conforming to the interface and its ancestors. A classifier may implement a number of interfaces.</p><p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"> In the Interface realization <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link></span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">, you can modify the current element and add various specifications to it. </span><br /><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">  </span><br /><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">To open the </span><span class="hcp1" style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">Interface</span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"> </span><span class="hcp1" style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">Realization</span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"> dialog box, choose the </span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">Specification</span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"> command from the Realization shortcut menu. </span><br /><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"> <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Interface_realization.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Interface realization" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> </span></p><p>In the <span class="hcp1">Interface</span> <span class="hcp1">Realization</span> <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link>, the <span class="hcp1">Implementing</span> <span class="hcp1">Classifier</span> property references the classifier that realizes the Interface to which it points.</p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136711102 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00708: Internal Transition

- page_id: `136711102`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136711102/Internal+Transition
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:13:24.802+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

In all other cases, the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Action' space='MD2024x'] label identifies the event that triggered the corresponding Action expression. These events are called Internal Transitions and are semantically equivalent to self transitions except that the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='State' space='MD2024x'] is neither exited nor re-entered. This means that the corresponding exit and entry actions are not performed.

#### TIP: Fork And Join

Fork And Join

For more information about defining transitions, read [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Fork and Join' space='MD2024x'].

Specify the Internal Transition in the Transition's [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024x'].

#### TIP: Fork And Join

Fork And Join

For a more detailed description of this dialog, read [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Transition' space='MD2024x'].

To create and specify an Internal Transition

In your project, if you have a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='State Machine diagram' space='MD2024x'] created, do the following:

1. Double-click the State or select Specification from the State's [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Shortcut menu' space='CDH185'] . The State's Specification Window opens.
2. Click the Internal Transitions group.
3. Click the Create button. The Transition's Specification window opens.
4. Specify an Internal Transition .
5. Click Close.

To remove an Internal Transition

1. Double-click the State or select Specification from the State's shortcut menu. The State's Specification Window opens.
2. Click the Internal Transitions group.
3. The Transition's Specification window opens.
4. Click Delete . Then, Click Close .

#### PANEL: Related Pages

Related Pages

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Formatting symbols' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='UML elements' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Action' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Event' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='State Machine diagram' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Fork and Join' space='MD2024x']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Transition' space='MD2024x']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136711103 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00709: Interruptible activity region

- page_id: `136711103`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136711103/Interruptible+activity+region
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:13:24.833+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

An Interruptable region contains activity nodes. When a token leaves an interruptable region via edges designated by the region as interrupting edges, all tokens and behaviours in the region are terminated.

Specify the Interruptible Activity Region in the Interruptible Activity Region [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024x'].

To make an edge as interrupting, simply select an edge and click the "lightning" button, as shown in the image bellow.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Mark edge as interrupting

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>An Interruptable region contains activity nodes. When a token leaves an interruptable region via edges designated by the region as interrupting edges, all tokens and behaviours in the region are terminated.</p><p>Specify the Interruptible Activity Region in the Interruptible Activity Region <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link>.</p><p>To make an edge as interrupting, simply select an edge and click the &quot;lightning&quot; button, as shown in the image bellow.</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="make_interrupting.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Interruptible activity region" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6>Mark edge as interrupting</h6>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136715667 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00710: Introducing Macro Engine

- page_id: `136715667`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715667/Introducing+Macro+Engine
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:17:41.720+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Macro Engine Documentation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Macro Engine (previously called Script Engine) in MagicDraw allows you to create your own macro (script) by using BeanShell, Groovy, JavaScript Rhino, or Jython. With Macro Engine, you can control everything that is allowed in Open API, for example, transforming and manipulating models. Learn more about open API of MagicDraw or other Cameo Suite modeling tool (for example, Cameo Systems Modeler) in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Developer Guide' space=''] and find sample macros in *<MagicDraw installation directory>/samples/product features/macros*.

Macro Engine comes with Professional, Architect, and Enterprise Editions starting from MagicDraw version 16.6 and later.

##### Changing Javascript Nashorn to Javascript Rhino

The Macro Engine supports Javascript Rhino. If you have any macros written in Javascript Nashorn, which is [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Deprecated JavaScript Nashorn' space=''], you can change it to Javascript Rhino.

To change to Javascript Rhino

- Click Tools > Macros > Change JS Nashorn to Rhino

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Macro Engine (previously called Script Engine) in MagicDraw allows you to create your own macro (script) by using BeanShell, Groovy, JavaScript Rhino, or Jython. With Macro Engine, you can control everything that is allowed in Open API, for example, transforming and manipulating models. Learn more about open API of MagicDraw or other Cameo Suite modeling tool (for example, Cameo Systems Modeler) in <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Developer Guide" /></ac:link> and find sample macros in <em>&lt;MagicDraw installation directory&gt;/samples/product features/macros</em>.</p><p>Macro Engine comes with Professional, Architect, and Enterprise Editions starting from MagicDraw version 16.6 and later.</p><h3>Changing Javascript Nashorn to Javascript Rhino</h3><p>The Macro Engine supports Javascript Rhino. If you have any macros written in Javascript Nashorn, which is <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Deprecated JavaScript Nashorn" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[deprecated]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, you can change it to Javascript Rhino.</p><p>To change to Javascript Rhino</p><hr /><ul><li>Click <strong>Tools</strong> &gt; <strong>Macros</strong> &gt; <strong>Change JS Nashorn to Rhino</strong></li></ul>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136714518 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00711: Introduction

- page_id: `136714518`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136714518/Introduction
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:16:38.940+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard > Template Creation Tutorial
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1355068225

1355068226

1355068226

This user guide demonstrates how to create a report template using Report Wizard and use it in MagicDraw. To make the most of Report Wizard, you need to understand Apache Velocity and MagicDraw elements. Report Wizard uses Apache Velocity, this means that you will need to know how to create a Velocity template and that every rule that applies to Velocity will also apply to MagicDraw Report Wizard.

Velocity is a Java-based template engine that processes templates and references Java objects to produce output documents. A basic Velocity template can contain static text, layouts, conditional statements, and placeholders for each referenced Java object. When a template is being read by Velocity, conditional statements will be processed and placeholders will be replaced with the value from the referenced Java objects. You can use Velocity, for example, to generate web pages, emails, and read *XML* documents, but with Report Wizard it is now possible to use Velocity to read other types of templates, such as Rich Text Format documents. This user guide covers some basic aspects of Velocity, MagicDraw elements, and Report Wizard directives.

1355068224

**Related page**

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9c5c469b-c11c-4cff-be12-1bba3a457112"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1355068225</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4ab531a4-acde-4318-b5c0-529b8d53f6b8"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1355068226</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="62f80a6f-7430-417b-a6c6-74f3b027fc2d"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1355068226</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>This user guide demonstrates how to create a report template using Report Wizard and use it in MagicDraw. To make the most of Report Wizard, you need to understand Apache Velocity and MagicDraw elements. Report Wizard uses Apache Velocity, this means that you will need to know how to create a Velocity template and that every rule that applies to Velocity will also apply to MagicDraw Report Wizard.</p><p>Velocity is a Java-based template engine that processes templates and references Java objects to produce output documents. A basic Velocity template can contain static text, layouts, conditional statements, and placeholders for each referenced Java object. When a template is being read by Velocity, conditional statements will be processed and placeholders will be replaced with the value from the referenced Java objects. You can use Velocity, for example, to generate web pages, emails, and read <em>XML</em> documents, but with Report Wizard it is now possible to use Velocity to read other types of templates, such as Rich Text Format documents. This user guide covers some basic aspects of Velocity, MagicDraw elements, and Report Wizard directives.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2794887a-6f98-48f2-9b85-a3f47c2851b8"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1355068224</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="4dc280e8-5e54-4745-a5b5-4e96822291c6" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136715306 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00712: INumberingAction interface

- page_id: `136715306`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715306/INumberingAction+interface
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:17:21.989+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Developer Guide > Custom elements numbering
- labels: ['auto-number', 'numbering-scheme', 'number-part']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

What if we want to customize the numbering of elements even further? Let's say we need to number uml:Activities depending on their *getStructuredNode()*value. By implementing the [*com.nomagic.magicdraw.autoid.INumberingAction*](http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/autoid/INumberingAction.html) interface, it is possible to create the numbering that follow arbitrary conditions, this comes of course at the cost of increased complexity. The implementation must calculate the values for the numbers all by itself.

There are two parts to be explained that pertain to this interface. The actual interface that provides access to customized numbering and an immutable support class which encapsulates the values as defined in the *AutoNumber* and the associated *NumberingScheme* objects.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**The *[INumberingAction](http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/autoid/INumberingAction.html)*Interface**. The interface has one method, which is invoked whenever a new *Element* is created or when **Create**or **Renumber**buttons are clicked in the **Element Numbering** dialog. This applies of course only if the Element type equals the type for which the *AutoNumber* has been customized. So at the time when a new element is created, this method receives a *java.util.List* with a single entry. On the other hand, when the user navigates the **Element Numbering**dialog, the method receives all elements that are currently listed in the right partition of the dialog. This implies that all elements have the same owner. Since this will give access to the complete Model Tree, it is possible to collect necessary information from other parts of the model and incorporate it. This method must return a mapping of each element together with the value that was generated for it. There is no need to number every element that the method receives. The Numbering Framework will register the generated values internally and set the property value for each element.

**The [*com.nomagic.magicdraw.autoid.NumberingInfo*](http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/autoid/NumberingInfo.html) support object.**This is an immutable Java Bean object which provides the implementer of the**[*INumberingAction*](http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/autoid/INumberingAction.html)interface with all the information as defined in the *AutoNumber* and *NumberingScheme* objects:

- [getName()](http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/autoid/NumberingInfo.html#getName--) returns the name of the AutoNumber .
- [isDefaultNumber()](http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/autoid/NumberingInfo.html#isDefaultNumber--) returns the AutoNumber.defaultNumber value.
- *getScheme()* returns the AutoNumber.numberingScheme value, which is the NumberingScheme used to number this type of element.
- [getProperty()](http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/autoid/NumberingInfo.html#getProperty--) returns the AutoNumber.numberedProperty value, which is the Property that will hold the value of the calculated number.
- [getTarget()](http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/autoid/NumberingInfo.html#getTarget--) returns the AutoNumber.getTarget() value, which is the type of the elements to be numbered.
- [isDisplayedProperty()](http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/autoid/NumberingInfo.html#isDisplayedProperty--) returns true, if the value of the above *getProperty()* is displayed in the GUI. An element type can have more than one numbered Property , but only one can be displayed on symbols or in the Element Tree.
- [getPrefix()](http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/autoid/NumberingInfo.html#getPrefix--) returns the prefix as modified by the user in the Element Numbering dialog, which can be different from the AutoNumber.getPrefix() value (see the following figure).
- [getSeparator()](http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/autoid/NumberingInfo.html#getSeparator--) returns the separator as modified by the user in the Element Numbering dialog. In the implemented Numbering Framework, this value will override all the NumberParts of a type separator (see the following figure).

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

For more information on how to create Numbering customizations with «AutoNumber», «NumberingScheme», and «NumberPart» elements, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with generic numbering mechanism' space='MD2024x'].

###### **Constraints**

A *NumberingScheme* that references an expression which implements this interface, can have one and only one *NumberPart*. Namely a *NumberPart* that references an [*INumberingAction*](http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/autoid/INumberingAction.html).

###### **Example #1**

We shall now proceed to show a Java class that generates numbers for UML activities depending on their *getStructuredNode()* value. Since this class implements the**[*INumberingAction*](http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/autoid/INumberingAction.html)interface, it should be set as the expression in the only *NumberPart* of this *NumberingScheme*.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**

We have created a few activities inside a sample *Package*. With the creation of each new activity, an ID is generated and assigned to it. We then add some *StructuredActivityNodes* into two of the given activities. Now, in order to assign *Numbers* to these *Activities* that includes the number of *StructuredActivityNodes* children, we have to open the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Element Numbering dialog' space='MD2024x']**Element Numbering**dialog and renumber the Activities. This will generate new numbers that reflect the ownership relation.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

Let's have a look at the source code. This is a very simple example, which differentiates between receiving one or many elements, and calculates the values accordingly. This class can be found in *com.nomagic.magicdraw.autoid.sample*.

```java
generateIds(List elements, NumberingInfo nInfo) {
            Map idMap = new HashMap();
            if (! elements.isEmpty()) {
 
                // the NumberingInfo object contains the values set in the customization
                String baseId = nInfo.getPrefix() + nInfo.getSeparator();
 
                // when creating an Activity, this will be called with a single element 
                if (elements.size() == 1) {
                    Element e = elements.iterator().next();
                    Class>[] types = new Class>[]{Activity.class};
                    Collection extends Element> acts = ModelHelper.getElementsOfType(e.getOwner(), types, false, true);
                    String id = baseId + (acts.size());
                    idMap.put(e, id);

 
                    // when renumbering in the dialog this will be called with multiple elements
                } else {
                    int counter = 1;
 
                    // we sort so that renumber will get the same order
                    // normally this would ask for a custom comparator class
                    // but it is left out for brevity's sake
                    Collections.sort(elements);
                    for (Element e : elements) {
                        if (e instanceof Activity) { 
                            Activity act = (Activity) e; 
                            String id = baseId + counter; 
                            counter ++;
                            Collection nodes = act.getStructuredNode();
 
                            // we simply attach the suffix '$node_' and the node count 
                            if (nodes != null && !nodes.isEmpty()) { 
                                id += "$node_" + nodes.size();
                            }
                            idMap.put(e, id);
                        }
                    }
                }
            }
            // returning the mapping of elements and ids, so that the framework can register them
            return idMap;
        }
    }]]>
```

###### **Example #2**

A more complicated example can be found the OpenAPI packages *com.nomagic.magicdraw.autoid* and *com.nomagic.magicdraw.autoid.custo*. It shows an implementation that creates numbers for elements connected through UML Relationships. The UML diagram shows the [*com.nomagic.magicdraw.autoid.custom.DirectedAssociationNumbering*](http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/autoid/custom/DirectedAssociationNumbering.html) class, which is customized to number element types that are connected by «DirectedAssociation» links.

The interested Reader should consult the source code, in order to learn how such an algorithm has been implemented.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

*Example of relationship numbering (fragment)*

**

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p>What if we want to customize the numbering of elements even further? Let's say we need to number uml:Activities depending on their <em>getStructuredNode() </em>value. By implementing the <a href="http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/autoid/INumberingAction.html"><em>com.nomagic.magicdraw.autoid.INumberingAction</em></a> interface, it is possible to create the numbering that follow arbitrary conditions, this comes of course at the cost of increased complexity. The implementation must calculate the values for the numbers all by itself.</p><p>There are two parts to be explained that pertain to this interface. The actual interface that provides access to customized numbering and an immutable support class which encapsulates the values as defined in the <em>AutoNumber</em> and the associated <em>NumberingScheme</em> objects.</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="example_of_Numberingaction_interface_and_numberingInfo_support_object.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="INumberingAction interface" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><div class="WordSection1"><p><strong>The <em><a href="http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/autoid/INumberingAction.html">INumberingAction </a></em>Interface</strong>. The interface has one method, which is invoked whenever a new <em>Element</em> is created or when <strong>Create </strong>or <strong>Renumber </strong>buttons are clicked in the <strong>Element Numbering</strong> dialog. This applies of course only if the Element type equals the type for which the <em>AutoNumber</em> has been customized. So at the time when a new element is created, this method receives a <em>java.util.List</em> with a single entry. On the other hand, when the user navigates the <strong>Element Numbering </strong>dialog, the method receives all elements that are currently listed in the right partition of the dialog. This implies that all elements have the same owner. Since this will give access to the complete Model Tree, it is possible to collect necessary information from other parts of the model and incorporate it. This method must return a mapping of each element together with the value that was generated for it. There is no need to number every element that the method receives. The Numbering Framework will register the generated values internally and set the property value for each element.</p><p><strong>The <a href="http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/autoid/NumberingInfo.html"><em>com.nomagic.magicdraw.autoid.NumberingInfo</em></a> support object. </strong>This is an immutable Java Bean object which provides the implementer of the<em> </em><a href="http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/autoid/INumberingAction.html"><em>INumberingAction</em> </a>interface with all the information as defined in the <em>AutoNumber</em> and <em>NumberingScheme</em> objects:</p><ul><li><em><a href="http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/autoid/NumberingInfo.html#getName--">getName()</a></em> returns the name of the <em>AutoNumber</em>.</li><li><em><a href="http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/autoid/NumberingInfo.html#isDefaultNumber--">isDefaultNumber()</a></em> returns the <em>AutoNumber.defaultNumber</em> value.</li><li><a href="http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/autoid/NumberingInfo.html#getScheme--"><em>getScheme()</em> </a>returns the <em>AutoNumber.numberingScheme</em> value, which is the <em>NumberingScheme</em> used to number this type of element.</li><li><em><a href="http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/autoid/NumberingInfo.html#getProperty--">getProperty()</a></em> returns the <em>AutoNumber.numberedProperty</em> value, which is the <em>Property</em> that will hold the value of the calculated number.</li><li><em><a href="http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/autoid/NumberingInfo.html#getTarget--">getTarget()</a></em> returns the <em>AutoNumber.getTarget()</em> value, which is the type of the elements to be numbered.</li><li><em><a href="http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/autoid/NumberingInfo.html#isDisplayedProperty--">isDisplayedProperty()</a></em> returns true, if the value of the above <a href="http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/autoid/NumberingInfo.html#getProperty--"><em>getProperty()</em></a> is displayed in the GUI. An element type can have more than one numbered <em>Property</em>, but only one can be displayed on symbols or in the Element Tree.</li><li><em><a href="http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/autoid/NumberingInfo.html#getPrefix--">getPrefix()</a></em> returns the prefix as modified by the user in the <strong style="line-height: 1.4285715;">Element Numbering </strong>dialog, which can be different from the <em>AutoNumber.getPrefix()</em> value (see the following figure).</li><li><em><a href="http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/autoid/NumberingInfo.html#getSeparator--">getSeparator()</a></em> returns the separator as modified by the user in the <strong style="line-height: 1.4285715;">Element Numbering </strong>dialog. In the implemented Numbering Framework, this value will override all the <em>NumberParts</em> of a type separator (see the following figure).</li></ul><p><ac:image ac:title="Modified Separator and Prefix values (fragment of Element Numbering dialog)" ac:alt="Modified Separator and Prefix values (fragment of Element Numbering dialog)"><ri:attachment ri:filename="modified_separator_and_prefix_values.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="INumberingAction interface" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p> </p><p>For more information on how to create Numbering customizations with «AutoNumber», «NumberingScheme», and «NumberPart» elements, <span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">see <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Working with generic numbering mechanism" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Working with Generic Numbering Mechanism]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</span></p><h4><strong>Constraints</strong></h4><p>A <em>NumberingScheme</em> that references an expression which implements this interface, can have one and only one <em>NumberPart</em>. Namely a <em>NumberPart</em> that references an <a href="http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/autoid/INumberingAction.html"><em>INumberingAction</em></a>.</p><h4><strong>Example #1</strong></h4><p>We shall now proceed to show a Java class that generates numbers for UML activities depending on their <em>getStructuredNode()</em> value. Since this class implements the<em> </em><a href="http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/autoid/INumberingAction.html"><em>INumberingAction</em> </a>interface, it should be set as the expression in the only <em>NumberPart</em> of this <em>NumberingScheme</em>.</p><p><ac:image ac:title="ActivityScheme numbering scheme (fragment)" ac:alt="ActivityScheme numbering scheme (fragment)"><ri:attachment ri:filename="activityScheme_numbering_scheme.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="INumberingAction interface" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p><em><br /></em></p><div class="WordSection1"><p>We have created a few activities inside a sample <em>Package</em>. With the creation of each new activity, an ID is generated and assigned to it. We then add some <em>StructuredActivityNodes</em> into two of the given activities. Now, in order to assign <em>Numbers</em> to these <em>Activities</em> that includes the number of <em>StructuredActivityNodes</em> children, we have to open the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Element Numbering dialog" /><ac:link-body><strong style="line-height: 1.4285715;">Element Numbering </strong>dialog</ac:link-body></ac:link> and renumber the Activities. This will generate new numbers that reflect the ownership relation.</p><p><ac:image ac:title="Activities and Structural Nodes before and after renumbering using Element Numbering dialog" ac:alt="Activities and Structural Nodes before and after renumbering using Element Numbering dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Activities and Structural Nodes before and after renumbering using Element Numbering dialog.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="INumberingAction interface" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></div><div class="WordSection1"><p> </p><p>Let's have a look at the source code. This is a very simple example, which differentiates between receiving one or many elements, and calculates the values accordingly. This class can be found in <em>com.nomagic.magicdraw.autoid.sample</em>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b687e352-9cd5-43f5-80d6-572301180f43"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[    public class ActivityNodeNumbering implements INumberingAction {
        @Override
        public Map<Element, String> generateIds(List<Element> elements, NumberingInfo nInfo) {
            Map<Element, String> idMap = new HashMap<Element, String>();
            if (! elements.isEmpty()) {
 
                // the NumberingInfo object contains the values set in the customization
                String baseId = nInfo.getPrefix() + nInfo.getSeparator();
 
                // when creating an Activity, this will be called with a single element 
                if (elements.size() == 1) {
                    Element e = elements.iterator().next();
                    Class<?>[] types = new Class<?>[]{Activity.class};
                    Collection<? extends Element> acts = ModelHelper.getElementsOfType(e.getOwner(), types, false, true);
                    String id = baseId + (acts.size());
                    idMap.put(e, id);

 
                    // when renumbering in the dialog this will be called with multiple elements
                } else {
                    int counter = 1;
 
                    // we sort so that renumber will get the same order
                    // normally this would ask for a custom comparator class
                    // but it is left out for brevity's sake
                    Collections.sort(elements);
                    for (Element e : elements) {
                        if (e instanceof Activity) { 
                            Activity act = (Activity) e; 
                            String id = baseId + counter; 
                            counter ++;
                            Collection<StructuredActivityNode> nodes = act.getStructuredNode();
 
                            // we simply attach the suffix '$node_' and the node count 
                            if (nodes != null && !nodes.isEmpty()) { 
                                id += "$node_" + nodes.size();
                            }
                            idMap.put(e, id);
                        }
                    }
                }
            }
            // returning the mapping of elements and ids, so that the framework can register them
            return idMap;
        }
    }]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"> </p></div><h4><strong>Example #2</strong></h4><p>A more complicated example can be found the OpenAPI packages <em>com.nomagic.magicdraw.autoid</em> and <em>com.nomagic.magicdraw.autoid.custo</em>. It shows an implementation that creates numbers for elements connected through UML Relationships. The UML diagram shows the <a href="http://jdocs.nomagic.com/2024x/com/nomagic/magicdraw/autoid/custom/DirectedAssociationNumbering.html"><em>com.nomagic.magicdraw.autoid.custom.DirectedAssociationNumbering</em></a> class, which is customized to number element types that are connected by «DirectedAssociation» links.</p><p>The interested Reader should consult the source code, in order to learn how such an algorithm has been implemented.</p><p><ac:image ac:height="400"><ri:attachment ri:filename="example_of_relationship_numbering.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="INumberingAction interface" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p><em>Example of relationship numbering (fragment)</em></p><p><em><br /></em></p></div></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p> </p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

The warning message will open, see the figure below, when the use of an invalid method from a variable is encountered during the report generation. In this example, the “**getStereotypeProperty**” method is not a valid method of the**$report** variable.

###### Invalid Method Reference Warning Message.

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136712852 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00715: Invalid Redefinition

- page_id: `136712852`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136712852/Invalid+Redefinition
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:15:01.767+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Model analysis > Validation > UML validation rules > UML correctness constraints rules
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Abbreviation**

InvRdf****

**Description**

This validation rule checks if a property redefines the inherited property of the owner classifier.

**Severity**

Error

**Constrained Element**

Property, Operation

**Solvers**

To fix this, do one of the following:

- Change the Redefined Property value to the appropriate one that is inherited, not owned.
- Remove Element solver - removes the invalid redefining property from the model.

**Example**

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
The element *Car* owns two attributes, *wheel* and *type*. The attribute*wheel*redefines the attribute *type*, causing the validation error. 
The error is resolved by changing the *wheel*attribute's **Redefined Property**value to the appropriate one, which is the *wheel*attribute, inherited from the element *Transport*.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><strong>Abbreviation</strong></p><p>InvRdf<strong><br /></strong></p><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>This validation rule checks if a property redefines the inherited property of the owner classifier.</p><p><strong>Severity</strong></p><p>Error</p><p><strong>Constrained Element</strong></p><p>Property, Operation</p><p><strong>Solvers</strong></p><p>To fix this, do one of the following:</p><ul><li>Change the <strong>Redefined Property </strong>value to the appropriate one that is inherited, not owned.</li><li><strong>Remove Element</strong> solver -  removes the invalid redefining property from the model.</li></ul><p><strong>Example</strong></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="invalid_redefinition.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Invalid Redefinition" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><span style="color: rgb(94,108,132);">The element <em>Car</em> owns two attributes, <em>wheel</em> and <em>type</em>. The attribute </span><span style="color: rgb(94,108,132);"><em>wheel</em> </span><span style="color: rgb(94,108,132);">redefines the attribute <em>type</em>, causing the validation error.<br />The error is resolved by changing the <em>wheel </em>attribute's <strong>Redefined Property </strong>value to the appropriate one, which is the <em>wheel </em>attribute, inherited from the element <em>Transport</em></span><span style="color: rgb(94,108,132);">.</span></h6>
````

## PAGE 00718: Invoking actions

- page_id: `136715104`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715104/Invoking+actions
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:17:05.852+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Developer Guide > Adding new functionality
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Actions can be invoked from:

| Main menu |  |
| --- | --- |
| Main toolbar |  |
| Diagram palette |  |
| Diagram toolbar |  |
| Browser shortcut menu |  |
| Diagram shortcut menu |  |
| Smart manipulator toolbar |  |
| Keyboard shortcuts | This action cannot be represented in GUI. Create a new action and assign a keyboard shortcut to invoke that action. |

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136715408 space=MD2024x version=3 -->
## PAGE 00719: Java Annotation Member

- page_id: `136715408`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715408/Java+Annotation+Member
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2024-01-30T12:54:55.106+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > Java Code Engineering > Java Mapping to UML > Mapping to UML Rules
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Example

431974293

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Example

431974296

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Example

431974295

A Java annotation member is mapped directly to the UML Operation owned by the interface stereotyped as «JavaAnnotation». This operation, by itself, can have the stereotype «JavaAnnotationMember». However, it is optional, unless specify a default value for it.

A Java annotation member type is mapped to a UML Type property of a UML Parameter with the “Return“ direction kind.

Annotation member mapping table:

| Java Element | UML element |
| --- | --- |
| Annotation member declaration | UML Operation with the stereotype «JavaAnnotationMember» (Optional), owned by a UML Interface with the stereotype «JavaAnnotation». |
| Annotation member name | UML Operation Name |
| Annotation member documentation | UML Operation Documentation |
| Annotation member type | Mapped to the UML Parameter type with the “return“ direction kind (resides in UML Operation parameters list) |
| Annotation member type modifier | Mapped to the MagicDraw specified property “Type Modifier“ |
| Default value | {JavaAnnotationMemberDefaultValue} tagged value of a «JavaAnnotationMember» stereotype. The stereotype is set on UML Operation |

#### TIP: Example

Example

Java source code

```java

```

UML model

431974292

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Mapping to UML Rules' space='']

This operation, by itself, can have the stereotype «JavaAnnotationMember». However, it is optional, unless specify a default value for it.</span></p><p>A Java annotation member type is mapped to a UML Type property of a UML Parameter with the “Return“ direction kind.</p><p><br /></p><p>Annotation member mapping table:</p><table class="wrapped"><colgroup><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Java Element</th><th>UML element</th></tr><tr><td>Annotation member declaration</td><td><p><span>UML Operation with the stereotype «JavaAnnotationMember» (Optional), </span>owned by a UML Interface with the stereotype «JavaAnnotation». </p></td></tr><tr><td>Annotation member name</td><td><p><span>UML Operation Name</span></p></td></tr><tr><td>Annotation member documentation</td><td><p><span>UML Operation Documentation</span></p></td></tr><tr><td>Annotation member type</td><td><p><span>Mapped to the UML Parameter type with the “return“ direction kind (resides in UML Operation parameters list)</span></p></td></tr><tr><td>Annotation member type modifier</td><td><p><span>Mapped to the MagicDraw specified property “Type Modifier“</span></p></td></tr><tr><td>Default value</td><td><p><span>{JavaAnnotationMemberDefaultValue} tagged value of a «JavaAnnotationMember» stereotype. The stereotype is set on UML Operation</span></p></td></tr></tbody></table><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6222f7c8-85f8-4edc-9875-549d33a27403"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Example</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Java source code</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="fb1c4bf0-261c-43a5-926c-ad1a3bf3a32e"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[/**
````html
* Comment of annotation
*/
public @interface Annotation
{
int id();
String name() default "[unassigned]";
}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p>UML model</p><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d75c20f7-bd08-41fa-93e5-10179835bd7a"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">431974292</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related Pages</strong>:</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="e7f9a940-0b39-4669-9c98-23240e70e7b6"><ac:parameter ac:name="page"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Mapping to UML Rules" /></ac:link></ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

## PAGE 00720: Java Annotation Type

- page_id: `136715406`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715406/Java+Annotation+Type
- version_number: 2

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Example

431959380

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Example

431959383

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Example

431959382

A Java annotation declaration is mapped directly to the UML Interface with the stereotype «JavaAnnotation». Annotation modifiers are mapped into UML Interface properties. Annotation members are mapped to the UML Interface operations with the stereotype «JavaAnnotationMember».

Annotation mapping table

| Java Element | UML element |
| --- | --- |
| Annotation declaration | UML Interface with stereotype «JavaAnnotation» (optional) |
| Annotation name | UML Interface name |
| Annotation documentation | UML Interface Documentation |
| Visibility modifier | UML Interface "Visibility" property |


## PAGE 00721: Java Annotations Usage

- page_id: `136715409`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715409/Java+Annotations+Usage
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-01-30T12:55:01.006+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > Java Code Engineering > Java Mapping to UML > Mapping to UML Rules
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Example

431991466

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Example

431991468

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Example

431991467

You can annotate Java elements. This annotation is mapped on top of the {JavaAnnotations} tagged value of the stereotype «JavaElement». «JavaElement» is a base stereotype for all stereotypes used in Java mapping, and can be used directly or with any other stereotype derived from it. Annotation is mapped as a simple string value.

#### TIP: Example

Example

Java source code****

```java

```

Annotation is mapped as a simple string value.</span></p><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8e6db0d0-9da5-43cb-b723-2b1292f810d1"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Example</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Java source code<strong> </strong></p><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="18651406-ca61-421f-a33d-e8b01c04c988"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[public class Test

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136715414 space=MD2024x version=1 -->
## PAGE 00722: Java CE Options

- page_id: `136715414`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715414/Java+CE+Options
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2023-09-20T18:17:28.978+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > Java Code Engineering
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Java CE options are visible every time you attempt to reverse source code. This section describes the **Resolve collection generic**option for Java-specific options. Other options are common for all languages and are described in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Reverse Options' space='MD2024x'].

##### Resolve collection generics option

By default, this option is selected in the **Reverse Options** dialog. Java introduced parameterized types in JLS 3 and added type variables to all Java collections. With reverse engineering, it is possible to find which type is in a Java collection and make associations directly to the contained type, instead of the Java collection.

Reverse engineering allows you to find a Java parameterized collection and retrieves the Java type used in a container. This type is set as a *Type* to the UML Property. The container type is set to the UML Property/Java language property “Container“ as a simple string.

#### TIP: Example

Example

**Source code sample**

```java
attribute; 
} ]]>
```

```text

```

UML Model

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Attribute type, retrieved from collection

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Collection type in UML Property specification

Note, that*$$type$$* shows where it should be in the lined UML Property type on code generation.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Java CE options are visible every time you attempt to reverse source code. This section describes the <strong>Resolve collection generic </strong>option for Java-specific options. Other options are common for all languages and are described in <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Reverse Options" /></ac:link>.</p><h3>Resolve collection generics option</h3><p>By default, this option is selected in the <strong>Reverse Options</strong> dialog. Java introduced parameterized types in JLS 3 and added type variables to all Java collections. With reverse engineering, it is possible to find which type is in a Java collection and make associations directly to the contained type, instead of the Java collection.</p><p>Reverse engineering allows you to find a Java parameterized collection and retrieves the Java type used in a container. This type is set as a <em>Type</em> to the UML Property. The container type is set to the UML Property/Java language property “Container“ as a simple string.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="517c7ceb-087e-49e9-b601-303c1bb3e9f5"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Example</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Source code sample</strong></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="bd0fec67-0cd6-494d-8598-ea0278a183c8"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[public class Test 
{ 
    java.util.List<String> attribute; 
} ]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><pre><br /></pre><p>UML Model</p><p><ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="69"><ri:attachment ri:filename="attribute_type_retrieved_from_collection.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Java CE Options" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6><span style="color: rgb(112,112,112);">Attribute type, retrieved from collection </span></h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="container_property.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024x" ri:content-title="Java CE Options" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></h6><h6 style="text-align: center;">Collection type in UML Property specification</h6><p>Note, that<em> $$type$$</em> shows where it should be in the lined UML Property type on code generation. </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136715395 space=MD2024x version=3 -->
## PAGE 00723: Java Class

- page_id: `136715395`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715395/Java+Class
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2024-02-01T11:10:39.946+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > Java Code Engineering > Java Mapping to UML > Mapping to UML Rules
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Example

431638635

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Example

431638638

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Example

431638636

A Java class is mapped directly to the UML [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class' space='']with the stereotype «JavaClass». This stereotype is optional and if the UML Class has no stereotype, Java CE treats it as a Java class. If no appropriate property is found in UML,class modifiers are mapped into UML Class properties or to the Java language properties for Class.

Java class fields, operations and inner classes are mapped to the appropriate UML Properties, UML Operations and UML Classes.

**Class mapping table**

| Java Element | UML element |
| --- | --- |
| Class declaration | UML Class with stereotype «JavaClass» (optional) |
| Class name | UML Class name |
| Class Documentation | UML Class Documentation |
| Class extends Clause | Mapped to the UML Generalization relationship, where the supplier is an extended class and the client is a mapped class |
| Class Implementation Clause | Mapped to the UML Interface Realization relationship, where the supplier is an extended class and the client is a mapped class. |
| Visibility Modifier | UML Class “Visibility” property |
| Abstract Modifier | UML Class “Is Abstract” property |
| Final Modifier | UML Class “Is Final Specialization“ property |
| Static Modifier | Java Language property “Static modifier“ |
| Strictfp Modifier | Java Language property “Strictfp modifier“ |

#### TIP: Example

Example

Java source code

```java

```

UML model

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Mapping to UML Rules' space='']

(optional) </span></p></td></tr><tr><td>Class name</td><td><p><span>UML Class name<br /></span></p></td></tr><tr><td>Class Documentation</td><td><p><span>UML Class class</span></p></td></tr><tr><td>Class Implementation Clause</td><td><p><span>Mapped to the UML Interface Realization relationship, where the supplier is an extended class and the client is a mapped class.</span></p></td></tr><tr><td>Visibility Modifier</td><td><p><span>UML Class “Visibility” property</span></p></td></tr><tr><td>Abstract Modifier</td><td><p><span>UML Class “Is Abstract” ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1be81bb5-b5e1-4f30-8a06-d3c627058e48"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Example</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Java source code</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a1723835-5b4a-4c83-8065-ffbb3d48b622"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">java</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[/**
````html
* Comment of the class MyList
*/
public final class MyList extends ArrayList implements Cloneable
{
}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target">UML model</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="class_diagram_ce_example.png" /></ac:image></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="bfdafbfb-fb68-4c14-9151-b283ba2ab778"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">431638634</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related Pages</strong>:</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="e7f9a940-0b39-4669-9c98-23240e70e7b6"><ac:parameter ac:name="page"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Mapping to UML Rules" /></ac:link></ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=136715386 space=MD2024x version=3 -->
## PAGE 00724: Java Code Engineering

- page_id: `136715386`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715386/Java+Code+Engineering
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2024-02-01T11:50:01.095+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1295392607

1295392609

1295392608

This chapter describes how our modeling tool maps Java language elements to UML, helps choose which profiles to use, and describes Java code engineering properties.

This chapter features the following:

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Java Mapping to UML' space=''] describes general rules our modeling tool uses to map each Java element to UML and which profile is used. You will find an example and corresponding model with marked properties used in Java to describe mapping rules.
2. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Java CE Options' space=''] introduces specific Java options.
3. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Method Implementation Reverse' space=''] describes a functionality that switches Java from a Sequence Diagram to our modeling tool.

##### References

Gosling, J., Joy, B., Steele, G., Bracha, G., & Buckley, A. (2012, February 6). The JavaTM Language Specifica- tion. Java SE 7 Edition. Retrieved March 29, 2012, from [http://docs.oracle.com/javase/specs/jls/se7/jls7.pdf](http://docs.oracle.com/javase/specs/jls/se7/jls7.pdf)

##### Java support in modeling tools

You can perform the following actions:

- Import Java source code into a model (reverse engineering).
- Generate Java code from the model (code generation).
- Apply changes to the source code from the model (round-trip). You can change Java declaration headers and apply them to existing source code; however, you cannot change the method of implementation.
- Create a sequence diagram from the selected method body.
- Create a model from the Java byte code.

1295392606

**Related pages**:

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Code Engineering' space='']

You will find an example and corresponding model with marked properties used in Java to describe mapping rules.</li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Java CE Options" /></ac:link> introduces specific Java options.</li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Method Implementation Reverse" /></ac:link> describes a functionality that switches Java from a Sequence Diagram to our modeling tool. </li></ol><h3><span>References</span></h3><p>Gosling, J., Joy, B., Steele, G., Bracha, G., &amp; Buckley, A. (2012, February 6). The JavaTM Language Specifica- tion. Java SE 7 Edition. Retrieved March 29, 2012, from <span style="color: rgb(90,109,148);"><a href="http://docs.oracle.com/javase/specs/jls/se7/jls7.pdf">http://docs.oracle.com/javase/specs/jls/se7/jls7.pdf</a></span></p><h3>Java support in modeling tools</h3><p>You can perform the following actions:</p><ul><li>Import Java source code into a model (reverse engineering).</li><li>Generate Java code from the model (code generation).</li><li>Apply changes to the source code from the model (round-trip). You can change Java declaration headers and apply them to existing source code; however, you cannot change the method of implementation.</li><li>Create a sequence diagram from the selected method body.</li><li>Create a model from the Java byte code. </li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7bfa9b55-1faf-45fa-8139-ae558c36a3f7"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1295392606</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong>:</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="7efc3ca5-b709-4690-8500-d1f74772044a" /></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="d8bafd44-21ad-4a13-a4ba-d00914ab9591"><ac:parameter ac:name="page"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Code Engineering" /></ac:link></ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

## PAGE 00725: Java Documentation Properties dialog box

- page_id: `136715381`
- space_key: `MD2024x`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024x/pages/136715381/Java+Documentation+Properties+dialog+box
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-01-30T15:02:26.541+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > Global options for Code Engineering
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

419370658

419370660

419370659

To open the **Java Documentation Properties**dialog box

1. Open the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Setting project options' space='MD2024x']**Project Options** dialog box .
2. In the Options list, expand Code Engineering and click Java Language Options .
3. In the Java Language Options area, select Java Doc as the Processor for Documentation .
4. Click [ATTACHMENT filename='three-dot-btn1.png'] to open the Documentation Properties dialog.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Documentation Properties dialog box

| Box Name | Function |
| --- | --- |
| Tag Name | Type the tag name. |
| Value | Type the value of the tag. |
| Generate | The selected tag will be placed in the generated code as a comment before a classifier (class or interface), operation or attribute. |
| Up | Moves the selected item up the list. |
| Down | Moves the selected item down the list. |
| Add | Adds a new item in the list. |
| Remove | Removes the selected from the list. |
| OK | Saves changes and closes the dialog box. |
| Cancel | Closes the dialog box without saving changes. |
| Help | Displays MagicDraw Help. |

419370657
